title.zh: 卷七十六 趙尹韓張兩王傳
title.en: Volume 76: Zhao, Yin, Han, Zhang and two Wangs
title.raw: 卷七十六 趙尹韓張兩王傳 Volume 76: Zhao, Yin, Han, Zhang and two Wangs

sentences.zh: 卷七十六 趙尹韓張兩王傳第四十六

translations.literal: Volume 76: "Biographies of Zhao, Yin, Han, Zhang, and the Two Wangs," number 46.
translations.idiomatic: This is Book 76 of the History of the Han: the forty-sixth set of biographies, devoted to Zhao Guanghan, Yin Wengui, Han Yanshou, Zhang Chang, Wang Zun, and Wang Jun.

sentences.zh: 趙廣漢字子都，涿郡蠡吾人也，故屬河間。

translations.literal: Zhao Guanghan, courtesy name Zidu, was from Liwu in Zhuo Commandery, which formerly belonged to Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Guanghan, styled Zidu, came from Liwu in Zhuo Commandery, in what had once been the kingdom of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 少為郡吏、州從事，以廉絜通敏下士為名。

translations.literal: In his youth he served as a commandery clerk and a provincial staff officer, and he was known for integrity, scrupulous conduct, quick intelligence, and respect for men of lower rank.
translations.idiomatic: While young he held posts as commandery clerk and provincial aide, earning a reputation for clean hands, sound judgment, and a habit of deferring to capable men beneath him in rank.

sentences.zh: 舉茂材，平準令。

translations.literal: He was recommended as outstanding talent and appointed Director of Equitable Marketing.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended on the "outstanding talent" roster, he was named Director of the Office of Equitable Marketing.

sentences.zh: 察廉為陽翟令。

translations.literal: Recommended for incorruptibility, he became magistrate of Yangdi.
translations.idiomatic: A "incorrupt and capable" nomination brought him the magistracy of Yangdi.

sentences.zh: 以治行尤異，遷京輔都尉，守京兆尹。

translations.literal: Because his administrative record was exceptionally distinguished, he was promoted to Metropolitan Commandant for the capital region and appointed acting Governor of the metropolitan area.
translations.idiomatic: His governance stood out so sharply that he rose to Metropolitan Commandant and served as acting Governor of the capital prefecture.

sentences.zh: 會昭帝崩，而新豐杜建為京兆掾，護作平陵方上。

translations.literal: It happened that Emperor Zhao died; Du Jian of Xinfeng was a clerk under the metropolitan Governor, supervising construction on the upper mound of Emperor Zhao's Pingling mausoleum.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhao died, Du Jian of Xinfeng—then a clerk in the metropolitan yamen—was put in charge of work on the tumulus at Pingling.

sentences.zh: 建素豪俠，賓客為姦利，廣漢聞之，先風告。

translations.literal: Jian had long been a swaggering bravo; his retainers pursued illicit gain. Guanghan heard of it and first sent him a quiet warning.
translations.idiomatic: Du Jian was a local strongman whose hangers-on ran rackets; Guanghan caught wind of it and tipped him off before taking action.

sentences.zh: 建不改，於是收案致法。

translations.literal: Jian did not change his ways, so Guanghan arrested him, built a case, and applied the law.
translations.idiomatic: When Du Jian ignored the warning, Guanghan had him seized, prosecuted, and punished under statute.

sentences.zh: 中貴人豪長者為請無不至，終無所聽。

translations.literal: Powerful figures at court and eminent local men pleaded on his behalf by every imaginable means, but Guanghan would not yield.
translations.idiomatic: Influential eunuchs and grandees lobbied relentlessly for Du Jian; Guanghan refused every approach.

sentences.zh: 宗族賓客謀欲篡取，廣漢盡知其計議主名起居，使吏告曰：「若計如此，且并滅家。」

translations.literal: Jian's kinsmen and retainers plotted to snatch him by force. Guanghan knew every ringleader's name and their comings and goings; he sent clerks to warn them: "If you go through with this, your whole clans will be wiped out."
translations.idiomatic: Kin and retainers plotted a rescue, but Guanghan already knew every ringleader; through his officers he sent word: "If you go through with this, your whole clans will be wiped out."

sentences.zh: 令數吏將建棄巿，莫敢近者。

translations.literal: He had several officers escort Jian to public execution in the market; no one dared come near.
translations.idiomatic: Several guards marched Du Jian to the execution ground in the marketplace, and nobody dared interfere.

sentences.zh: 京師稱之。

translations.literal: The capital praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Word of it spread through the capital, and people praised him.

sentences.zh: 是時，昌邑王徵即位，行淫亂，大將軍霍光與群臣共廢王，尊立宣帝。

translations.literal: At that time the King of Changyi had been summoned to the throne; his conduct was dissolute. Grand General Huo Guang, with the ministers, deposed the king and raised Emperor Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Changyi had just been enthroned and was running wild; Grand General Huo Guang and the court deposed him and set Emperor Xuan on the throne.

sentences.zh: 廣漢以與議定策，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Guanghan took part in deliberating the succession and was enfeoffed as a marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: For his role in fixing the new reign, Guanghan received the noble rank of marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 遷潁川太守。

translations.literal: He was transferred to Governor of Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: He was then posted as Governor of Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 郡大姓原、褚宗族橫恣，賓客犯為盜賊，前二千石莫能禽制。

translations.literal: The great clans surnamed Yuan and Chu dominated the commandery; their retainers turned to robbery and banditry, and none of the previous chiefs at two thousand piculs had been able to seize or curb them.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuan and Chu lineages bullied the region; their clients committed theft and violence, and one governor after another had failed to bring them to heel.

sentences.zh: 廣漢既至數月，誅原、褚首惡，郡中震栗。

translations.literal: Within a few months of Guanghan's arrival he executed the worst offenders among the Yuan and Chu; the commandery shook with fear.
translations.idiomatic: A few months after he arrived, he executed the chief villains of both houses, and the whole commandery trembled.

sentences.zh: 先是，潁川豪桀大姓相與為婚姻，吏俗朋黨。

translations.literal: Before that, the powerful families of Yingchuan had intermarried until they formed a tight web; clerks and local custom alike were tangled in faction.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, the great houses had married one another into a single bloc, while officials and local habit alike were bound up in cliques.

sentences.zh: 廣漢患之，厲使其中可用者受記，出有案問，既得罪名，行法罰之，廣漢故漏泄其語，令相怨咎。

translations.literal: Guanghan worried over this. He urged certain usable men among them to file sealed denunciations, then sent officers out to follow up; once guilt was fixed he punished them by law—but he deliberately leaked who had informed, so the clans turned on one another.
translations.idiomatic: He broke the deadlock by encouraging selected insiders to register complaints, then investigated each lead—but he also leaked the informers' identities so the magnates blamed each other instead of him.

sentences.zh: 又教吏為缿筩，及得投書，削其主名，而託以為豪桀大姓子弟所言。

translations.literal: He also had clerks set up complaint boxes; when letters arrived he erased the writers' names and pretended the tips came from sons and nephews of other powerful houses.
translations.idiomatic: He set up sealed post boxes for accusations, then stripped signatures from the letters and circulated them as if they had been penned by rival magnates' younger kin.

sentences.zh: 其後彊宗大族家家結為仇讎，姦黨散落，風俗大改。

translations.literal: Afterward every strong lineage nursed a private feud with its neighbors; criminal cliques fell apart, and local custom changed radically.
translations.idiomatic: Soon great families were at each other's throats, criminal networks dissolved, and the moral climate of the commandery shifted overnight.

sentences.zh: 吏民相告訐，廣漢得以為耳目，盜賊以故不發，發又輒得。

translations.literal: Clerks and commoners denounced one another, giving Guanghan a network of informers; banditry therefore rarely broke out, and when it did the culprits were quickly taken.
translations.idiomatic: Informants multiplied, so Guanghan always had ears in the streets; crime rarely surfaced, and when it did arrests followed fast.

sentences.zh: 壹切治理，威名流聞，及匈奴降者言匈奴中皆聞廣漢。

translations.literal: Order was restored in every quarter; his reputation for awe-inspiring justice spread far, and even surrendered Xiongnu said that everyone beyond the frontier had heard of Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: His stern justice became famous even on the steppe: Xiongnu who had come over to the Han reported that his name was known in every camp.

sentences.zh: 本始二年，漢發五將軍擊匈奴，徵廣漢以太守將兵，屬蒲類將軍趙充國。

translations.literal: In the second year of the Benshi era the Han sent five generals against the Xiongnu; Guanghan was called up to lead troops as Governor, serving under General Zhao Chongguo of the Pu-lei column.
translations.idiomatic: In 72 B.C. the court launched five columns against the Xiongnu; Guanghan marched at the head of his commandery contingent under Zhao Chongguo's Pu-lei command.

sentences.zh: 從軍還，復用守京兆尹，滿歲為真。

translations.literal: When the army returned he was again named acting metropolitan Governor, and after a full year the appointment was confirmed.
translations.idiomatic: After the campaign he resumed acting duty as Governor of the capital; a year later the post was made permanent.

sentences.zh: 廣漢為二千石，以和顏接士，其尉薦待遇吏，殷勤甚備。

translations.literal: As a two-thousand-picul official Guanghan received gentlemen with an open, kindly manner; in recommending clerks for promotion and in how he treated them he was painstakingly generous.
translations.idiomatic: Though he held rank at two thousand piculs, he met subordinates without airs and took unusual care with promotions and day-to-day kindness to his staff.

sentences.zh: 事推功善，歸之於下，曰：「某掾卿所為，非二千石所及。」

translations.literal: When something went well he pushed the credit down the line, saying, "So-and-so clerk did this—it is beyond what a chief at two thousand piculs could have done himself."
translations.idiomatic: He habitually credited his aides: "That was so-and-so's work, not mine," he would say, even when the achievement was substantial.

sentences.zh: 行之發於至誠。

translations.literal: He did this out of genuine sincerity.
translations.idiomatic: None of it was theater; his men felt it was heartfelt.

sentences.zh: 吏見者皆輸寫心腹，無所隱匿，咸願為用，僵仆無所避。

translations.literal: Clerks who met him poured out their inmost thoughts, hid nothing, and were eager to serve him, even to lay down their lives without hesitation.
translations.idiomatic: Officers who dealt with him laid their plans bare and volunteered for the dirtiest work without flinching.

sentences.zh: 廣漢聰明，皆知其能之所宜，盡力與否。

translations.literal: Guanghan was sharp-witted; he knew which tasks suited each man's talents and whether each was giving his full effort.
translations.idiomatic: He read people quickly, matched talent to duty, and knew who was slacking.

sentences.zh: 其或負者，輒先聞知，風諭不改，乃收捕之，無所逃，按之罪立具，即時伏辜。

translations.literal: If someone fell short, Guanghan usually heard of it first; he would hint a warning, and only if the man failed to mend his ways did he arrest him—there was no escape; once the case was built the confession came at once and punishment followed immediately.
translations.idiomatic: Slackers got a private warning first; if they ignored it he moved in, and his interrogations were so tight that guilt surfaced the same day.

sentences.zh: 廣漢為人彊力，天性精於吏職。

translations.literal: Guanghan was forceful by nature and had an instinctive gift for administration.
translations.idiomatic: He was physically tough and born to the work of a frontier magistrate.

sentences.zh: 見吏民，或夜不寢至旦。

translations.literal: In dealing with clerks and commoners he sometimes went without sleep from dusk to dawn.
translations.idiomatic: He would interview petitioners through the night until cockcrow.

sentences.zh: 尤善為鉤距，以得事情。

translations.literal: He was especially skilled at "hook-and-gauge" inquiry to get at the facts.
translations.idiomatic: His specialty was the cross-checking method later called "hook and gauge."

sentences.zh: 鉤距者，設欲知馬賈，則先問狗，已問羊，又問牛，然後及馬，參伍其賈，以類相準，則知馬之貴賤不失實矣。

translations.literal: "Hook and gauge" means: if you want to know the price of a horse, you first ask the price of a dog, then of a sheep, then of an ox, and only then of the horse; you triangulate the figures by category, and thus learn the horse's true market level without error.
translations.idiomatic: The idea was to circle the truth: ask about dogs, sheep, and cattle before you ask about horses, compare the numbers, and the real price of the horse emerges from the pattern.

sentences.zh: 唯廣漢至精能行之，它人效者莫能及也。

translations.literal: Only Guanghan refined the technique to perfection; others who tried to copy him never matched him.
translations.idiomatic: Others imitated the trick, but none wielded it with his precision.

sentences.zh: 郡中盜賊，閭里輕俠，其根株窟穴所在，及吏受取請求銖兩之姦，皆知之。

translations.literal: He knew where every bandit in the commandery and every neighborhood bravo had his hideout, and he knew clerks' bribe-taking down to the weight of a few zhu.
translations.idiomatic: He could map every thief's den in the capital and trace petty corruption in the yamen to the last coin.

sentences.zh: 長安少年數人會窮里空舍謀共劫人，坐語未訖，廣漢使吏捕治具服。

translations.literal: Several young men of Chang'an met in a derelict house in a poor lane to plan a robbery; before they had finished talking Guanghan's officers burst in, arrested them, and secured full confessions.
translations.idiomatic: A gang of youths was still mid-plot in a slum tenement when his men kicked the door and had full confessions before dawn.

sentences.zh: 富人蘇回為郎，二人劫之。

translations.literal: The rich man Su Hui held the post of gentleman-at-court; two men seized him as a hostage.
translations.idiomatic: Su Hui, a wealthy resident who served as a court gentleman, was taken hostage by two robbers.

sentences.zh: 有頃，廣漢將吏到家，自立庭下，使長安丞襲奢叩堂戶曉賊，曰：「京兆尹趙君謝兩卿，無得殺質，此宿衛臣也。

translations.literal: Presently Guanghan led officers to the house, stood himself in the courtyard, and had the assistant magistrate of Chang'an, Xi She, knock on the hall door and call to the bandits: "The metropolitan Governor Zhao thanks you two gentlemen: do not kill the hostage—he is an imperial bodyguard on duty."
translations.idiomatic: Guanghan took his place in the courtyard and had Xi She knock at the hall door and call to the kidnappers: "Metropolitan Governor Zhao thanks you both—do not kill the hostage; he is an imperial guardsman on duty."

sentences.zh: 釋質，束手，得善相遇，幸逢赦令，或時解脫。」

translations.literal: "Release your hostage, bind your own hands, and you will be treated well; if an amnesty comes you may yet go free."
translations.idiomatic: Release the captive, tie your own wrists, and you will be handled fairly—perhaps even freed under a future amnesty.

sentences.zh: 二人驚愕，又素聞廣漢名，即開戶出，下堂叩頭，廣漢跪謝曰：「幸全活郎，甚厚！」

translations.literal: The two were stunned; they had long heard Guanghan's name. They opened the door, came downstairs, and kowtowed. Guanghan knelt in return and said, "It is a great relief that the gentleman is safe!"
translations.idiomatic: They opened the door, came down, and kowtowed; Guanghan knelt with them and said, "It is a great relief that the gentleman is safe!"

sentences.zh: 送獄，敕吏謹遇，給酒肉。

translations.literal: He sent them to jail, ordered the jailers to treat them kindly, and supplied wine and meat.
translations.idiomatic: He committed them to prison with orders for decent food and drink.

sentences.zh: 至冬當出死，豫為調棺，給斂葬具，告語之，皆曰：「死無所恨！」

translations.literal: When winter came and they were due for execution, he arranged coffins and burial goods in advance, told them plainly, and they said, "We die without regret!"
translations.idiomatic: When winter came he told them plainly of the coffins he had provided; each answered, "We die without regret!"

sentences.zh: 廣漢嘗記召湖都亭長，湖都亭長西至界上，界上亭長戲曰：「至府，為我多謝問趙君。」

translations.literal: Guanghan once issued a written summons to the chief of the Hu capital post station; the man rode west to the commandery border, where the border post chief joked, "When you reach the yamen, give my regards to Governor Zhao."
translations.idiomatic: He summoned the Hu post chief, who met a colleague at the border; the man joked, "When you reach the yamen, give Governor Zhao my regards."

sentences.zh: 亭長既至，廣漢與語，問事畢，謂曰：「界上亭長寄聲謝我，何以不為致問？」

translations.literal: When the post chief arrived Guanghan finished his business, then said, "The border post chief sent his regards through you—why did you not pass them on?"
translations.idiomatic: When the chief arrived, Guanghan finished his business, then said, "The border chief sent his regards through you—why did you not pass them on?"

sentences.zh: 亭長叩頭服實有之。

translations.literal: The post chief kowtowed and admitted it was true.
translations.idiomatic: The officer confessed at once.

sentences.zh: 廣漢因曰：「還為吾謝界上亭長，勉思職事，有以自效，京兆不忘卿厚意。」

translations.literal: Guanghan said, "On your way back thank the border chief for me: urge him to mind his duties and find ways to prove himself—the metropolitan Governor appreciates his good will."
translations.idiomatic: Guanghan added, "On your way back thank the border chief for me: urge him to mind his duties and find ways to prove himself—the metropolitan Governor has not forgotten his courtesy."

sentences.zh: 其發姦擿伏如神，皆此類也。

translations.literal: His knack for exposing crime and bringing hidden plots to light was almost supernatural—this was typical.
translations.idiomatic: Stories of that sort were endless; people said he could smell a lie before it was spoken.

sentences.zh: 廣漢奏請，令長安游徼獄吏秩百石，其後百石吏皆差自重，不敢枉法妄繫留人。

translations.literal: Guanghan memorialized that patrol officers and jail clerks of Chang'an be given rank of one hundred piculs; afterward holders of that rank took themselves more seriously and dared not twist the law or detain people without cause.
translations.idiomatic: He won hundred-picul rank for patrolmen and jailers so they would not be bullied into illegal arrests.

sentences.zh: 京兆政清，吏民稱之不容口。

translations.literal: Metropolitan government grew clean; clerks and people praised him without pause.
translations.idiomatic: Under him the capital administration ran clean, and praise ran from mouth to mouth.

sentences.zh: 長老傳以為自漢興以來治京兆者莫能及。

translations.literal: Elders handed down the judgment that since the founding of the Han no metropolitan Governor had surpassed him.
translations.idiomatic: Old men said no capital governor since Gaozu had matched him.

sentences.zh: 左馮翊、右扶風皆治長安中，犯法者從跡喜過京兆界。

translations.literal: The governors of Left Feng-yi and Right Fu-feng both had jurisdiction inside Chang'an, so criminals liked to slip across the line into the metropolitan district.
translations.idiomatic: Because the two "assistant" prefects also policed the city, wrongdoers often darted into Guanghan's jurisdiction to dodge them.

sentences.zh: 廣漢歎曰：「亂吾治者，常二輔也！

translations.literal: Guanghan sighed, "What undoes my work is always those two assistants!"
translations.idiomatic: Guanghan sighed, "What undoes my work is always those two adjunct prefects!"

sentences.zh: 誠令廣漢得兼治之，直差易耳。」

translations.literal: "If I were allowed to govern all three together, the job would be far easier."
translations.idiomatic: Give me all three offices, he said, and order would be simple.

sentences.zh: 初，大將軍霍光秉政，廣漢事光。

translations.literal: Earlier Grand General Huo Guang had held the levers of power, and Guanghan had served under him.
translations.idiomatic: In Huo Guang's years of dominance Guanghan had been his man.

sentences.zh: 及光薨後，廣漢心知微指，發長安吏自將，與俱至光子博陸侯禹第，直突入其門，廋索私屠酤，椎破盧罌，斧斬其門關而去。

translations.literal: After Guang died Guanghan sensed the political wind, mobilized Chang'an officers, marched with them to the mansion of Guang's son, the Marquis of Bolu, Yu, forced the gate, searched for illegal private brewing and butchery, smashed jars and urns, axed the door bolt, and left.
translations.idiomatic: When Huo Guang died, Guanghan read the court, led a party straight into Huo Yu's Bolu mansion, hunted for illicit breweries, smashed equipment, and split the gate hasp with an axe—then walked away.

sentences.zh: 時光女為皇后，聞之，對帝涕泣。

translations.literal: At that time Huo Guang's daughter was empress; when she heard, she wept before the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Huo's daughter was empress; she wept until the emperor took notice.

sentences.zh: 帝心善之，以召問廣漢。

translations.literal: The emperor sympathized with her and summoned Guanghan for questioning.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign felt for her and called Guanghan in for an explanation.

sentences.zh: 廣漢由是侵犯貴戚大臣。

translations.literal: From then on Guanghan rode roughshod over imperial in-laws and great ministers.
translations.idiomatic: After that he treated even consort kin and senior statesmen as fair game.

sentences.zh: 所居好用世吏子孫新進年少者，專厲彊壯鋒氣，見事風生，無所回避，率多果敢之計，莫為持難。

translations.literal: In the offices he preferred sons of established clerk families—young men newly promoted—whom he drilled to be hard, sharp, and swift; they seized every case as if riding a gale, shrank from nothing, favored bold strokes, and seldom paused to weigh risk.
translations.idiomatic: He filled his staff with aggressive young men from clerkly families, trained them to strike fast and ask questions later, and discouraged second thoughts.

sentences.zh: 廣漢終以此敗。

translations.literal: Guanghan was ruined in the end by that very habit.
translations.idiomatic: That reckoning would destroy him.

sentences.zh: 初，廣漢客私酤酒長安巿，丞相史逐去客。

translations.literal: Earlier a client of Guanghan's had sold ale illegally in the Chang'an market; a chancellor's clerk drove him off.
translations.idiomatic: Long before, a client who brewed bootleg wine in the market had been chased off by the chancellor's office.

sentences.zh: 客疑男子蘇賢言之，以語廣漢。

translations.literal: The client suspected a man named Su Xian had informed on him and told Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: The client blamed Su Xian and told Guanghan.

sentences.zh: 廣漢使長安丞按賢，尉史禹故劾賢為騎士屯霸上，不詣屯所，乏軍興。

translations.literal: Guanghan had the assistant magistrate of Chang'an investigate Xian; the sheriff's clerk Yu trumped up a charge that Xian, a mounted trooper stationed at Bashang, had failed to report to camp—an offense under the statutes on military exigency.
translations.idiomatic: Guanghan set the Chang'an assistant on Su Xian; a clerk named Yu framed him for absenting himself from cavalry duty at Bashang.

sentences.zh: 賢父上書訟罪，告廣漢，事下有司覆治。

translations.literal: Xian's father submitted a memorial disputing the verdict and accusing Guanghan; the case was sent down for reinvestigation.
translations.idiomatic: Su Xian's father appealed, implicating Guanghan, and the file went back to the regular agencies.

sentences.zh: 禹坐要斬，請逮捕廣漢。

translations.literal: Yu was sentenced to waist bisection and requested Guanghan's arrest.
translations.idiomatic: Yu drew a capital sentence and demanded Guanghan be seized as well.

sentences.zh: 有詔即訊，辭服，會赦，貶秩一等。

translations.literal: An edict ordered immediate interrogation; Guanghan confessed; there was an amnesty; he was demoted one grade in rank.
translations.idiomatic: An imperial order forced a hearing; Guanghan admitted fault, then caught an amnesty that only stripped one rank.

sentences.zh: 廣漢疑其邑子榮畜教令，後以它法論殺畜。

translations.literal: Guanghan suspected his fellow townsman Rong Chu had put Xian up to the appeal and later had Chu condemned and executed on another charge.
translations.idiomatic: He suspected Rong Chu, a townsman, of coaching the suit and later had Chu executed on an unrelated charge.

sentences.zh: 人上書言之，事下丞相御史，案驗甚急。

translations.literal: Someone memorialized about it; the matter went to the chancellor and imperial counsellor, and investigation pressed hard.
translations.idiomatic: Word reached the chancellor and censor; the scrutiny tightened.

sentences.zh: 廣漢使所親信長安人為丞相府門卒，令微司丞相門內不法事。

translations.literal: Guanghan planted a trusted Chang'an man as gate guard at the chancellor's mansion with orders to spy for illegalities inside.
translations.idiomatic: He slipped an agent into the chancellor's gate watch to listen for dirt.

sentences.zh: 地節三年七月中，丞相傅婢有過，自絞死。

translations.literal: In the seventh month of the third year of Dijie a maidservant raised in the chancellor's household offended her master and hanged herself.
translations.idiomatic: That summer a foster maid in the Wei household hanged herself after a beating.

sentences.zh: 廣漢聞之，疑丞相夫人妒殺之府舍。

translations.literal: When Guanghan heard, he guessed the chancellor's wife had murdered her out of jealousy in the official residence.
translations.idiomatic: Guanghan assumed the wife had murdered her out of jealousy.

sentences.zh: 而丞相奉齋酎入廟祠，廣漢得此，使中郎趙奉壽風曉丞相，欲以脅之，毋令窮正己事。

translations.literal: The chancellor was fasting with ritual ale before entering the ancestral temple; Guanghan seized the moment, sent Gentleman of the Household Zhao Fengshou to hint to the chancellor, and tried to blackmail him into dropping the case against himself.
translations.idiomatic: While Wei Xiang was fasting for the imperial sacrifice, Guanghan sent Zhao Fengshou with a veiled threat: drop the probe of Guanghan, or else.

sentences.zh: 丞相不聽，按驗愈急。

translations.literal: The chancellor refused to listen, and the investigation grew more urgent.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang ignored the pressure and doubled the effort to clear his own house.

sentences.zh: 廣漢欲告之，先問太史知星氣者，言今年當有戮死大臣，廣漢即上書告丞相罪。

translations.literal: Guanghan planned to strike first; he consulted the court astrologer, who said a great minister would die by execution this year; Guanghan immediately submitted a memorial accusing the chancellor of a capital crime.
translations.idiomatic: He asked the star readers whether a high minister would die violently that year, then filed capital charges against Wei Xiang.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「下京兆尹治。」

translations.literal: The rescript read: "Send it to the metropolitan Governor for trial."
translations.idiomatic: The rescript read: "Send the case down to the metropolitan Governor for trial."

sentences.zh: 廣漢知事迫切，遂自將吏卒突入丞相府，召其夫人跪庭下受辭，收奴婢十餘人去，責以殺婢事。

translations.literal: Guanghan knew time was short; he personally led clerks and soldiers into the chancellor's mansion, had Lady Wei kneel in the courtyard for questioning, seized more than ten servants, and pressed the charge of murdering the maid.
translations.idiomatic: He stormed the chancellor's compound, forced Wei's wife to give testimony in the courtyard, and dragged off a dozen slaves as witnesses.

sentences.zh: 丞相魏相上書自陳：「妻實不殺婢。

translations.literal: Chancellor Wei Xiang submitted a memorial in his own defense: "My wife did not kill the maid."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang wrote in his own defense, "My wife did not kill the maid."

sentences.zh: 廣漢數犯罪法不伏辜，以詐巧迫脅臣相，幸臣相寬不奏。

translations.literal: "Guanghan has repeatedly violated the law without accepting punishment; with deceit and coercion he pressures your subject Xiang; only Xiang's leniency has kept him from being reported."
translations.idiomatic: "Guanghan has repeatedly broken the law without paying for it; he twists the statutes and pressures your subject Wei—only Wei's patience has kept this from being reported."

sentences.zh: 願下明使者治廣漢所驗臣相家事。」

translations.literal: "I beg that a clear-sighted envoy be dispatched to try what Guanghan claims to have proved about your subject's household."
translations.idiomatic: "I beg that a clear-sighted envoy be sent to test what Guanghan claims he proved about your subject's household."

sentences.zh: 事下廷尉治罪，實丞相自以過譴笞傅婢，出至外弟乃死，不如廣漢言。

translations.literal: The case went to the commandant of justice: in fact the chancellor had caned the maid for an offense; she left and died at a younger brother's house—not as Guanghan had alleged.
translations.idiomatic: The commandant of justice found that Wei had whipped the girl, she fled to a brother's house, and died there—nothing like Guanghan's story.

sentences.zh: 司直蕭望之劾奏：「廣漢摧辱大臣，欲以劫持奉公，逆節傷化，不道。」

translations.literal: Director of Integrity Xiao Wang-zhi impeached Guanghan: "He humiliates great ministers, seeks to hold hostage one who serves the state, reverses proper order, and wounds moral transformation—conduct lacking the Way."
translations.idiomatic: Xiao Wang-zhi impeached him for terrorizing a chief minister and subverting public order.

sentences.zh: 宣帝惡之，下廣漢廷尉獄，又坐賊殺不辜，鞠獄故不以實，擅斥除騎士乏軍興數罪。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan detested this and sent Guanghan to the commandant of justice's prison; he was also convicted of murdering the innocent, falsifying trial records, and unlawfully stripping cavalrymen of duty—several crimes including military-exigency violations.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, disgusted, jailed him on multiple counts: judicial fraud, wrongful death, and abuse of military law.

sentences.zh: 天子可其奏。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven approved the findings.
translations.idiomatic: The throne accepted the verdict.

sentences.zh: 吏民守闕號泣者數萬人，或言「臣生無益縣官，願代趙京兆死，使得牧養小民。」

translations.literal: Several tens of thousands of clerks and commoners gathered at the palace gate, weeping; some said, "Our lives are useless to the state—we beg to die in Governor Zhao's place so he may continue to shepherd the people."
translations.idiomatic: Tens of thousands mobbed the palace gate, weeping; some cried, "Our lives are useless to the state—we beg to die in Governor Zhao's place so he may go on protecting the little people."

sentences.zh: 廣漢竟坐要斬。

translations.literal: Guanghan was in the end sentenced to waist bisection and executed.
translations.idiomatic: He went to the block nonetheless.

sentences.zh: 廣漢雖坐法誅，為京兆尹廉明，威制豪彊，小民得職。

translations.literal: Though Guanghan died by the law, as metropolitan Governor he was incorrupt and clear-sighted, awed the mighty, and let humble folk keep their livelihoods.
translations.idiomatic: Even after his execution, memory lingered of a governor who faced down magnates and gave commoners a fair shake.

sentences.zh: 百姓追思，歌之至今。

translations.literal: The people remember him still and sing of him to this day.
translations.idiomatic: Ballads about him are still sung.

sentences.zh: 尹翁歸字子兄，河東平陽人也，徙杜陵。

translations.literal: Yin Wengui, courtesy name Zixiong, was from Pingyang in Hedong and had moved his household to Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Wengui, styled Zixiong, was a native of Pingyang in Hedong who later settled at Duling.

sentences.zh: 翁歸少孤，與季父居。

translations.literal: Wengui was orphaned young and lived with a younger paternal uncle.
translations.idiomatic: Orphaned early, he was raised by an uncle.

sentences.zh: 為獄小吏，曉習文法。

translations.literal: He served as a minor jail clerk and mastered statutes and precedent.
translations.idiomatic: He began as a petty jail clerk and memorized the code.

sentences.zh: 喜擊劍，人莫能當。

translations.literal: He delighted in swordsmanship; no one could stand against him.
translations.idiomatic: He loved fencing and had few peers with the blade.

sentences.zh: 是時大將軍霍光秉政，諸霍在平陽，奴客持刀兵入巿鬥變，吏不能禁，及翁歸為巿吏，莫敢犯者。

translations.literal: At that time Grand General Huo Guang held power; the Huo clan was at Pingyang; slaves and retainers entered the market with weapons, fought, and rioted; clerks could not stop them—but when Wengui became market clerk none dared offend.
translations.idiomatic: While the Huo family swaggered through Pingyang, armed slaves brawled in the market until Yin Wengui took the market office—after which nobody dared make trouble.

sentences.zh: 公廉不受餽，百賈畏之。

translations.literal: Upright and incorruptible, he accepted no gifts; the merchants feared him.
translations.idiomatic: He took no bribes, and the guilds walked wide of him.

sentences.zh: 後去吏居家。

translations.literal: Later he left office and lived at home.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned and lived privately for a time.

sentences.zh: 會田延年為河東太守，行縣至平陽，悉召故吏五六十人，延年親臨見，令有文者東，有武者西。

translations.literal: When Tian Yannian became Governor of Hedong and on his inspection circuit reached Pingyang, he summoned fifty or sixty former clerks; Yannian personally oversaw the roll call and ordered those with literary talent to the east side and those with military talent to the west.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Yannian toured Pingyang as governor of Hedong, he lined up five or six dozen old clerks, civil men to the left, military types to the right.

sentences.zh: 閱數十人，次到翁歸，獨伏不肯起，對曰：「翁歸文武兼備，唯所施設。」

translations.literal: He had reviewed several dozen; when it came to Wengui alone, Wengui lay prone and refused to rise, saying, "Wengui combines civil and military skills—employ me as you will."
translations.idiomatic: When his turn came, Wengui alone stayed prone and said, "Wengui combines civil and military skills—employ me as you will."

sentences.zh: 功曹以為此吏倨敖不遜，延年曰：「何傷？」

translations.literal: The merit assessor thought this clerk arrogant and disrespectful; Yannian said, "What harm is there?"
translations.idiomatic: Yannian only laughed and asked, "What harm is there?"

sentences.zh: 遂召上辭問，甚奇其對，除補卒史，便從歸府。

translations.literal: Thereupon he summoned Wengui to come forward, questioned him on his replies, marveled greatly at his answers, appointed him a clerk-scribe, and had him accompany him back to the yamen.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian called him up, quizzed him, and was so struck by his answers that he named him a clerk-scribe on the spot and took him home to the governor's compound.

sentences.zh: 案事發姦，窮竟事情，延年大重之，自以能不及翁歸，徙署督郵。

translations.literal: In handling cases he exposed crime and pursued matters to the end; Yannian thought very highly of him, felt his own ability did not match Wengui's, and transferred him to serve as surveillance inspector.
translations.idiomatic: Wengui broke cases wide open; Yannian rated him above himself and moved him to the inspector's post.

sentences.zh: 河東二十八縣，分為兩部，閎孺部汾北，翁歸部汾南。

translations.literal: Hedong's twenty-eight counties were split into two circuits: Hongru had the north of the Fen River, Wengui the south.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery was halved for inspection duty—Hongru rode the north bank of the Fen, Wengui the south.

sentences.zh: 所舉應法，得其罪辜，屬縣長吏雖中傷，莫有怨者。

translations.literal: His denunciations matched the law and hit the guilty mark; though county magistrates whom he impeached felt wronged, none harbored resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Even magistrates he ruined admitted his charges were fair.

sentences.zh: 舉廉為緱氏尉，歷守郡中，所居治理，遷補都內令，舉廉為弘農都尉。

translations.literal: Recommended as incorrupt he became sheriff of Goushi, held acting posts in the commandery, wherever he went order prevailed; he was promoted to director of the metropolitan granary, then recommended as incorrupt to commandant of Hongnong.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from Goushi sheriff through acting county seats to capital granary chief, then to Hongnong as an "incorrupt" nominee.

sentences.zh: 徵拜東海太守，過辭廷尉于定國。

translations.literal: He was summoned and appointed governor of the Eastern Sea; en route he paid a farewell visit to Commandant of Justice Yu Dingguo.
translations.idiomatic: The court named him governor of the Eastern Sea; he stopped to take leave of Yu Dingguo at the commandant's office.

sentences.zh: 定國家在東海，欲屬託邑子兩人，令坐後堂待見。

translations.literal: Dingguo's family estate lay in the Eastern Sea; he wished to entrust two fellow-townsmen to Wengui and had them wait in the rear hall for an audience.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Dingguo wanted a favor for two locals from his home county and parked them in the back hall.

sentences.zh: 定國與翁歸語終日，不敢見其邑子。

translations.literal: Dingguo talked with Wengui all day and dared not bring in his townsmen.
translations.idiomatic: Yu talked with Wengui until nightfall and never dared introduce the pair.

sentences.zh: 既去，定國乃謂邑子曰：「此賢將，汝不任事也，又不可干以私。」

translations.literal: After Wengui left, Dingguo told his townsmen: "This is a worthy governor; you are not fit for office, and private pleas cannot sway him."
translations.idiomatic: After Wengui left, Dingguo told his townsmen, "That is a worthy governor; you are not fit for office, and private pleas cannot move him."

sentences.zh: 翁歸治東海明察，郡中吏民賢不肖，及姦邪罪名盡知之。

translations.literal: Wengui governed the Eastern Sea with piercing clarity; he knew every clerk and commoner, worthy or base, and every charge of wickedness.
translations.idiomatic: In the Eastern Sea he knew the moral ledger of every household.

sentences.zh: 縣縣各有記籍。

translations.literal: Each county kept its own register.
translations.idiomatic: Every district had a dossier on its people.

sentences.zh: 自聽其政，有急名則少緩之；

translations.literal: When he personally heard cases, if a name on the list was urgent he deliberately slowed the matter;
translations.idiomatic: Anyone on his hot list found the docket mysteriously quiet until he was ready to move.

sentences.zh: 吏民小解，輒披籍。

translations.literal: and when clerks and people relaxed their guard, he would open the registers.
translations.idiomatic: When clerks and commoners relaxed, he reopened the registers and closed the trap.

sentences.zh: 縣縣收取黠吏豪民，案致其罪，高至於死。

translations.literal: County by county he seized crafty clerks and powerful commoners, built cases, and punished up to death.
translations.idiomatic: He swept up corrupt underlings and bullies and prosecuted them to the block when the evidence warranted.

sentences.zh: 收取人必於秋冬課吏大會中，及出行縣，不以無事時。

translations.literal: He always made arrests at the great autumn-winter review of clerks or when touring counties—never idly in peacetime.
translations.idiomatic: Roundups came only at the annual clerk muster or on inspection tours, never as casual harassment.

sentences.zh: 其有所取也，以一警百，吏民皆服，恐懼改行自新。

translations.literal: When he acted, it was to warn a hundred with one example; clerks and people submitted in fear and reformed.
translations.idiomatic: One high-profile arrest taught the whole commandery a lesson.

sentences.zh: 東海大豪郯許仲孫為姦猾，亂吏治，郡中苦之。

translations.literal: Xu Zhongsun of Tan, a great magnate of the Eastern Sea, was cunning and threw the administration into chaos; the commandery suffered under him.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Zhongsun of Tan was the sort of bravo who owned the local yamen.

sentences.zh: 二千石欲捕者，輒以力勢變詐自解，終莫能制。

translations.literal: Governors who tried to arrest him he always evaded by force and fraud; none could control him.
translations.idiomatic: Every previous governor had found him untouchable.

sentences.zh: 翁歸至，論棄仲孫巿，一郡怖栗，莫敢犯禁。

translations.literal: When Wengui arrived he sentenced Zhongsun to public execution in the market; the whole commandery trembled and none dared break the law.
translations.idiomatic: Wengui had Xu strung up in the marketplace, and overnight the law meant something again.

sentences.zh: 東海大治。

translations.literal: The Eastern Sea was brought to great order.
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Sea became a model of quiet streets.

sentences.zh: 以高第入守右扶風，滿歲為真。

translations.literal: By top evaluation he entered as acting Right Governor of the Metropolitan area; after a full year the appointment was confirmed.
translations.idiomatic: He scored highest on the merit list and took acting charge of Right Fufeng; a year later the post was his in full.

sentences.zh: 選用廉平疾姦吏以為右職，接待以禮，好惡與同之；

translations.literal: He chose incorrupt, even clerks who hated wickedness for senior posts, received them with courtesy, and shared their likes and dislikes;
translations.idiomatic: He picked honest hard-liners for the top desks, treated them with respect, and stood with them on what to punish—

sentences.zh: 其負翁歸，罰亦必行。

translations.literal: whoever betrayed Wengui was inevitably punished.
translations.idiomatic: Yet anyone who betrayed Yin Wengui paid the price without exception.

sentences.zh: 治如在東海故跡，姦邪罪名亦縣縣有名籍。

translations.literal: His administration followed the same pattern as in the Eastern Sea; charges of wickedness too were listed county by county.
translations.idiomatic: He ran Fufeng the way he had run the coast, with a file on every crooked deal in every county.

sentences.zh: 盜賊發其比伍中，翁歸輒召其縣長吏，曉告以姦黠主名，教使用類推跡盜賊所過抵，類常如翁歸言，無有遺託。

translations.literal: When bandits struck within a mutual-responsibility group, Wengui would summon that county's magistrate, name the ringleaders, and teach him to track by analogy where the thieves had gone; it always matched Wengui's prediction, with nothing left out.
translations.idiomatic: If a theft rang the neighborhood alarm, he called the magistrate in, fed him the names of local fences, and told him how to trace the trail—and he was never wrong.

sentences.zh: 緩於小弱，急於豪彊。

translations.literal: He was lenient toward the small and weak, harsh toward the powerful.
translations.idiomatic: The poor got mercy; magnates got the law.

sentences.zh: 豪彊有論罪，輸掌畜官，使斫莝，責以員程，不得取代。

translations.literal: When magnates were convicted he handed them to the livestock office to chop fodder, set quotas, and forbade substitutes.
translations.idiomatic: Convicted grandees chopped hay under the granary steward at fixed daily quotas, with no stand-ins.

sentences.zh: 不中程，輒笞督，極者至以鈇自剄而死。

translations.literal: Missing the quota brought flogging; in extreme cases some cut their own throats with the executioner's axe.
translations.idiomatic: Short weight meant the rod; a few killed themselves with the court axe rather than face another round.

sentences.zh: 京師畏其威嚴，扶風大治，盜賊課常為三輔最。

translations.literal: The capital feared his severity; Fufeng was well governed; his theft-clearance figures regularly ranked first among the Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: The metropolis heard his name with respect; crime statistics in his district led the capital region year after year.

sentences.zh: 翁歸為政雖任刑，其在公卿之間清絜自守，語不及私，然溫良嗛退，不以行能驕人，甚得名譽於朝廷。

translations.literal: Though Wengui relied on punishments in office, among high ministers he kept clean reserve and never spoke of private matters; he was gentle, modest, and never arrogant about his abilities, winning great repute at court.
translations.idiomatic: He was a hangman with a scholar's manners: severe on the bench, soft-spoken in the capital, never trading on his talent.

sentences.zh: 視事數歲，元康四年病卒。

translations.literal: After several years in office he died of illness in the fourth year of Yuankang.
translations.idiomatic: He fell ill and died in 62 B.C., only a few years into the Fufeng post.

sentences.zh: 家無餘財，天子賢之，制詔御史：「朕夙興夜寐，以求賢為右，不異親疏近遠，務在安民而已。

translations.literal: His house held no surplus wealth; the emperor thought him worthy and issued an edict to the imperial secretary: "We rise early and sleep late, taking the search for worthy men as foremost, without distinction of kinship or distance, aiming only to settle the people.
translations.idiomatic: He died poor; the emperor told the censor: "I lose sleep over finding good officials, kin or stranger, if only the people may rest.

sentences.zh: 扶風翁歸廉平鄉正，治民異等，早夭不遂，不得終其功業，朕甚憐之。

translations.literal: Wengui of Fufeng was incorrupt, fair, and upright in his village; his governance of the people stood apart; he died young and could not finish his work—We pity him deeply.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Wengui of Fufeng was clean, straight, and a singular governor; cut off before his prime, he leaves unfinished business that grieves Us."

sentences.zh: 其賜翁歸子黃金百斤，以奉其祭祠。」

translations.literal: Grant his son one hundred jin of gold for sacrifices."
translations.idiomatic: Give his son a hundred catties of gold for the ancestral rites."

sentences.zh: 翁歸三子皆為郡守。

translations.literal: All three of Wengui's sons became governors.
translations.idiomatic: Each of his three boys rose to a commandery governorship.

sentences.zh: 少子岑歷位九卿，至後將軍。

translations.literal: The youngest, Cen, rose through the Nine Ministers to rear general.
translations.idiomatic: Cen, the youngest, climbed to the Nine Ministers and ended as rear general.

sentences.zh: 而閎孺亦至廣陵相，有治名。

translations.literal: Hongru too reached chancellor of Guangling with a reputation for good administration.
translations.idiomatic: His old partner Hongru made chancellor of Guangling with a name for competence.

sentences.zh: 由是世稱田延年為知人。

translations.literal: Hence the world praised Tian Yannian for knowing how to judge men.
translations.idiomatic: People said Tian Yannian had an eye for talent.

sentences.zh: 韓延壽字長公，燕人也，徙杜陵。

translations.literal: Han Yanshou, courtesy name Changgong, was from Yan and had moved his household to Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yanshou, styled Changgong, was a Yan man who settled at Duling.

sentences.zh: 少為郡文學。

translations.literal: In youth he served as commandery erudite-clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He began as the commandery's literary officer.

sentences.zh: 父義為燕郎中。

translations.literal: His father Yi was a court gentleman of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: His father Han Yi held a gentleman post at the Yan court.

sentences.zh: 剌王之謀逆也，義諫而死，燕人閔之。

translations.literal: When the Prince of Guangling plotted rebellion, Yi remonstrated and was killed; the people of Yan pitied him.
translations.idiomatic: When the Prince of Guangling turned traitor, Han Yi spoke out and died for it; Yan mourned him.

sentences.zh: 是時昭帝富於春秋，大將軍霍光持政，徵郡國賢良文學，問以得失。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhao was still young; Grand General Huo Guang held power and summoned worthy erudites from commanderies and kingdoms to ask about policy gains and losses.
translations.idiomatic: Under the boy emperor, Huo Guang called in local scholars to debate what had gone wrong in the realm.

sentences.zh: 時魏相以文學對策，以為「賞罰所以勸善禁惡，政之本也。

translations.literal: At that time Wei Xiang answered the examination as an erudite, saying that "rewards and punishments encourage good and restrain evil—the root of government.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang answered the examination, "Rewards and punishments encourage good and restrain evil—that is the root of government."

sentences.zh: 日者燕王為無道，韓義出身彊諫，為王所殺。

translations.literal: Lately the King of Yan acted lawlessly; Han Yi stepped forward to remonstrate strongly and was killed by the king.
translations.idiomatic: The Prince of Yan had murdered Han Yi for daring to rebuke treason.

sentences.zh: 義無比干之親而蹈比干之節，宜顯賞其子，以示天下，明為人臣之義。」

translations.literal: Yi had no tie of kinship like Bi Gan yet walked in Bi Gan's path; his son should be openly rewarded to show the empire what it means to serve a lord."
translations.idiomatic: Han Yi was no royal uncle, yet he matched Bi Gan's courage; honor his son and the world will see what loyalty costs."

sentences.zh: 光納其言，因擢延壽為諫大夫，遷淮陽太守。

translations.literal: Guang accepted the advice, promoted Yanshou to grandee remonstrant, and transferred him to governor of Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang agreed and pulled Yanshou up to remonstrant, then sent him to Huaiyang as governor.

sentences.zh: 治甚有名，徙潁川。

translations.literal: His administration was very famous and he was shifted to Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: His reputation there earned him Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 潁川多豪彊，難治，國家常為選良二千石。

translations.literal: Yingchuan had many powerful houses and was hard to govern; the court often chose excellent chiefs for it.
translations.idiomatic: Yingchuan's magnates made it a posting that tested every governor.

sentences.zh: 先是，趙廣漢為太守，患其俗多朋黨，故構會吏民，令相告訐，一切以為聰明，潁川由是以為俗，民多怨讎。

translations.literal: Earlier Zhao Guanghan, as governor, had worried over the prevalence of cliques and therefore set clerks and people at one another's throats with mutual denunciations, taking everything as cleverness; Yingchuan took this as custom and the people nursed many feuds.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Guanghan had broken the cliques by turning neighbor against neighbor; the habit of spying lingered and hatreds piled up.

sentences.zh: 延壽欲更改之，教以禮讓，恐百姓不從，乃歷召郡中長老為鄉里所信向者數十人，設酒具食，親與相對，接以禮意，人人問以謠俗，民所疾苦，為陳和睦親愛銷除怨咎之路。

translations.literal: Yanshou wished to change this and teach courtesy and yielding, but feared the people would not follow; he therefore summoned several dozen elders trusted in their villages, set out wine and food, sat with them in person, treated them with ritual respect, asked each about local songs, popular hardships, and explained the path of harmony, kinship love, and wiping away grudges.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou meant to replace denunciation with ritual: he feasted the village heads, heard their grievances, and preached reconciliation until they believed him.

sentences.zh: 長老皆以為便，可施行，因與議定嫁娶喪祭儀品，略依古禮，不得過法。

translations.literal: The elders all thought it workable; they then agreed on ritual standards for weddings, funerals, and sacrifices, broadly following ancient rites without exceeding statute.
translations.idiomatic: They drew up wedding, funeral, and sacrifice rules from old texts, kept within the code, and endorsed the plan.

sentences.zh: 延壽於是令文學校官諸生皮弁執俎豆，為吏民行喪嫁娶禮。

translations.literal: Yanshou thereupon ordered the school erudites and students, in leather caps bearing sacrificial stands and vessels, to perform wedding and funeral rites for clerks and commoners.
translations.idiomatic: He put the schoolboys in caps and gowns to stage public weddings and funerals by the book.

sentences.zh: 百姓遵用其教，賣偶車馬下里偽物者，棄之巿道。

translations.literal: The people followed his teaching; hawkers of fake carriages and shoddy suburban goods abandoned them in the streets.
translations.idiomatic: Vendors of paper hearses and cheap grave goods cleared their stock from the roads.

sentences.zh: 數年，徙為東郡太守，黃霸代延壽居潁川，霸因其跡而大治。

translations.literal: After several years he was transferred to governor of the Eastern Commandery; Huang Ba replaced him in Yingchuan and on his foundations achieved great order.
translations.idiomatic: A few years later he left for the Eastern Commandery; Huang Ba inherited his methods and finished the job in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 延壽為吏，上禮義，好古教化，所至必聘其賢士，以禮待用，廣謀議，納諫爭；

translations.literal: As an official Yanshou honored ritual and loved ancient moral transformation; wherever he went he always hired worthy gentlemen, treated them with courtesy, widened counsel, and accepted remonstrance;
translations.idiomatic: Han Yanshou governed as a Confucian reformer: he patronized scholars, welcomed remonstrance, and spread ritual education in every county he touched.

sentences.zh: 舉行喪讓財，表孝弟有行；

translations.literal: he promoted those who conducted funerals with yielding over property and singled out filial piety and fraternal duty where conduct deserved it;
translations.idiomatic: He singled out men who yielded property at funerals and sons famous for genuine filial conduct, then paraded them as models.

sentences.zh: 修治學官，春秋鄉社，陳鍾鼓管弦，盛升降揖讓，及都試講武，設斧鉞旌旗，習射御之事。

translations.literal: he repaired the school halls, at spring and autumn village sacrifices set out bells, drums, and pipes with solemn ascent, descent, bows, and yielding, and at the capital review for military drill displayed axes, halberds, and banners, practicing archery and charioteering.
translations.idiomatic: He rebuilt the schoolhouses, held spring and autumn village sacrifices with bells, drums, and pipes, and drilled the militia beneath halberds and banners until the commandery looked like a small army on parade.

sentences.zh: 治城郭，收賦租，先明布告其日，以期會為大事，吏民敬畏趨鄉之。

translations.literal: He repaired city walls, collected taxes and rents, first posted clear notice of the dates, and treated the appointed gatherings as great affairs; clerks and people stood in awe and hurried to comply.
translations.idiomatic: Tax days were posted in advance and kept sacred; nobody missed a deadline.

sentences.zh: 又置正、五長，相率以孝弟，不得舍姦人。

translations.literal: He also appointed village heads and group leaders of five households to lead one another in filial piety and fraternal duty and not harbor criminals.
translations.idiomatic: He tied hamlets together under headmen who vouched for mutual virtue.

sentences.zh: 閭里仟佰有非常，吏輒聞知，姦人莫敢入界。

translations.literal: If anything unusual happened in a lane or hamlet, clerks heard of it at once and villains dared not enter his borders.
translations.idiomatic: Any whisper in a ward reached the yamen the same day; crooks stayed out of his commandery.

sentences.zh: 其始若煩，後吏無追捕之苦，民無箠楚之憂，皆便安之。

translations.literal: At first it seemed burdensome, but later clerks had no chasing to do and the people no fear of the rod; all found it convenient and settled.
translations.idiomatic: The paperwork looked heavy at first, yet soon there were no manhunts and no beatings in the courtyard.

sentences.zh: 接待下吏，恩施甚厚而約誓明。

translations.literal: In receiving subordinate clerks he was very generous with kindness yet clear in sworn rules.
translations.idiomatic: He spoiled his staff with favors but wrote the rules in stone.

sentences.zh: 或欺負之者，延壽痛自刻責：「豈其負之，何以至此？」

translations.literal: If someone betrayed that trust, Yanshou bitterly blamed himself: "Have I failed them—how did it come to this?"
translations.idiomatic: When a clerk betrayed that trust, Yanshou bitterly blamed himself: "Have I failed them—how did it come to this?"

sentences.zh: 吏聞者自傷悔，其縣尉至自刺死。

translations.literal: Clerks who heard this were moved to shame and regret; one county sheriff went so far as to stab himself to death.
translations.idiomatic: One deputy was so ashamed he opened his own belly.

sentences.zh: 及門下掾自剄，人救不殊，因瘖不能言。

translations.literal: A household retainer also cut his throat; people saved him but he could not speak, having lost his voice.
translations.idiomatic: A gate clerk tried suicide and survived mute.

sentences.zh: 延壽聞之，對掾史涕泣，遣吏毉治視，厚復其家。

translations.literal: When Yanshou heard, he wept before his clerks, sent a physician to treat him, and richly compensated his family.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou wept in court, paid a doctor, and pensioned the family.

sentences.zh: 延壽嘗出，臨上車，騎吏一人後至，敕功曹議罰白。

translations.literal: Once when Yanshou was about to go out and had mounted his carriage, one mounted clerk arrived late; he ordered the merit assessor to deliberate punishment and report.
translations.idiomatic: One morning a mounted escort was late; Yanshou told the merit clerk to draw up a fine.

sentences.zh: 還至府門，門卒當車，願有所言。

translations.literal: Returning to the yamen gate, a gate guard blocked the carriage and begged to speak.
translations.idiomatic: At the gate a watchman stopped the horses.

sentences.zh: 延壽止車問之，卒曰：「孝經曰：『資於事父以事君，而敬同，故母取其愛，而君取其敬，兼之者父也。』

translations.literal: Yanshou halted and asked; the guard said: "The Classic of Filial Piety says: 'By the service with which one serves a father one serves a ruler, and the reverence is the same; therefore a mother is shown love, a ruler reverence, and he who combines both is the father.'
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou reined in; the watchman said, "The Classic of Filial Piety teaches that the reverence one shows a ruler matches the reverence one shows a father."

sentences.zh: 今旦明府早駕，久駐未出，騎吏父來至府門，不敢入。

translations.literal: This morning the governor's carriage waited long and had not departed; the mounted clerk's father came to the gate but dared not enter.
translations.idiomatic: That morning the governor's carriage had waited long at the gate; the rider's father had come but dared not interrupt.

sentences.zh: 騎吏聞之，趨走出謁，適會明府登車。

translations.literal: The mounted clerk heard, ran out to greet him, and it happened just as the governor mounted the carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Hearing his name, the son ran out to greet his father just as the governor called for the horses.

sentences.zh: 以敬父而見罰，得毋虧大化乎？」

translations.literal: To punish him for honoring his father—would that not harm great moral transformation?"
translations.idiomatic: Would you punish a man for filial haste, and teach the whole commandery to fear honoring a parent?"

sentences.zh: 延壽舉手輿中曰：「微子，太守不自知過。」

translations.literal: Yanshou raised his hand from the carriage and said: "Without you, the governor would not have known his fault."
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou lifted his hand: "Without you I would have blundered."

sentences.zh: 歸舍，召見門卒。

translations.literal: Back at his residence he summoned the gate guard.
translations.idiomatic: At home he sent for the watchman.

sentences.zh: 卒本諸生，聞延壽賢，無因自達，故代卒，延壽遂待用之。

translations.literal: The guard had originally been a student who, hearing Yanshou was worthy, had no way to present himself, so he substituted as a guard; Yanshou thereupon employed him.
translations.idiomatic: The fellow was a scholar who had taken gate duty just to meet him; Yanshou put him on staff.

sentences.zh: 其納善聽諫，皆此類也。

translations.literal: His acceptance of good counsel was all of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: That was how he took advice.

sentences.zh: 在東郡三歲，令行禁止，斷獄大減，為天下最。

translations.literal: In three years in the Eastern Commandery his orders were executed and his prohibitions observed; criminal cases dropped sharply and his district ranked first in the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Three years in the Eastern Commandery cut the docket to nothing and put him at the top of the empire's scorecard.

sentences.zh: 入守左馮翊，滿歲稱職為真。

translations.literal: He entered as acting Left Governor of Pingyi and after a full year of competent service was confirmed.
translations.idiomatic: He moved to acting Left Pingyi and won confirmation after a year.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，不肯出行縣。

translations.literal: For more than a year he refused to tour the counties.
translations.idiomatic: For over a year he would not ride circuit.

sentences.zh: 丞掾數白：「宜循行郡中，覽觀民俗，考長吏治跡。」

translations.literal: Assistant magistrates repeatedly urged: "You ought to make the rounds of the commandery, observe local custom, and examine magistrates' records of rule."
translations.idiomatic: His deputies begged him to inspect the counties and judge the magistrates.

sentences.zh: 延壽曰：「縣皆有賢令長，督郵分明善惡於外，行縣恐無所益，重為煩擾。」

translations.literal: Yanshou said: "Each county already has a worthy magistrate; the surveillance inspector sorts good from evil outside the walls—touring the counties would bring no benefit and would only add vexation."
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou replied, "Each county already has a worthy magistrate; the surveillance inspector sorts good from evil beyond the walls—a grand tour would bring no gain and would only vex the people."

sentences.zh: 丞掾皆以為方春月，可壹出勸耕桑。

translations.literal: The assistants all thought that in the spring month he might at least go out once to encourage farming and mulberry.
translations.idiomatic: They pressed him to bless the spring planting.

sentences.zh: 延壽不得已，行縣至高陵，民有昆弟相與訟田自言，延壽大傷之，曰：「幸得備位，為郡表率，不能宣明教化，至令民有骨肉爭訟，既傷風化，重使賢長吏、嗇夫、三老、孝弟受其恥，咎在馮翊，當先退。」

translations.literal: Yanshou had no choice; on his circuit he reached Gaoling, where brothers were suing each other over fields in person; Yanshou was deeply grieved and said: "Having obtained a place, I am the model for the commandery; I have failed to spread moral teaching, until the people bring flesh-and-blood suits before the court—this wounds custom and moreover makes worthy magistrates, village chiefs, elders, and exemplars of filial piety bear shame; the fault lies with the Governor of Pingyi, who should withdraw first."
translations.idiomatic: At Gaoling he found two brothers suing each other over land and said in anguish, "I hold this post as the commandery's model, yet I have failed to spread moral teaching until flesh and blood sue in open court—this shames custom and makes worthy magistrates, village chiefs, elders, and exemplars of filial piety bear disgrace; the fault lies with the Governor of Pingyi, who should withdraw first."

sentences.zh: 是日移病不聽事，因入臥傳舍，閉閤思過。

translations.literal: That day he pleaded illness and heard no business, then went to lie in the relay hostel, closed his doors, and reflected on his faults.
translations.idiomatic: He shut the yamen, took to the inn sickbed, and fasted in self-reproach.

sentences.zh: 一縣莫知所為，令丞、嗇夫、三老亦皆自繫待罪。

translations.literal: The whole county did not know what to do; magistrate, assistant, village chief, and elders all bound themselves and awaited punishment.
translations.idiomatic: The county officers tied their own hands and waited for the axe.

sentences.zh: 於是訟者宗族傳相責讓，此兩昆弟深自悔，皆自髡肉袒謝，願以田相移，終死不敢復爭。

translations.literal: Thereupon the litigants' clans passed blame among themselves; the two brothers repented deeply, shaved their heads, bared their shoulders in apology, offered to shift the fields to each other, and swore never to quarrel again.
translations.idiomatic: The clans shamed the brothers until they shaved their heads, stripped to the waist, and deeded the land back and forth in tears.

sentences.zh: 延壽大喜，開閤延見，內酒肉與相對飲食，厲勉以意告鄉部，有以表勸悔過從善之民。

translations.literal: Yanshou was greatly pleased, opened his doors to receive them, brought in wine and meat, ate and drank with them, and earnestly charged the village offices to use this as a model to encourage people who repented and turned to good.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou opened his doors, feasted them, and told every hamlet to copy the story.

sentences.zh: 延壽乃起聽事，勞謝令丞以下，引見尉薦。

translations.literal: Yanshou then rose to hear business, thanked magistrates and assistants downward, and received the sheriff's recommendations.
translations.idiomatic: He went back to work, thanked his staff, and held promotions court.

sentences.zh: 郡中歙然，莫不傳相敕厲，不敢犯。

translations.literal: The commandery fell silent; none failed to pass the lesson along and none dared offend.
translations.idiomatic: Word of the scene ran through every district; nobody wanted to be the next headline.

sentences.zh: 延壽恩信周遍二十四縣，莫復以辭訟自言者。

translations.literal: Yanshou's grace and trust spread through all twenty-four counties; none again brought suits on their own initiative.
translations.idiomatic: Within a season his twenty-four counties stopped filing petty suits.

sentences.zh: 推其至誠，吏民不忍欺紿。

translations.literal: Pushed by his utmost sincerity, clerks and people could not bear to deceive him.
translations.idiomatic: Sincerity that deep left no room for lies.

sentences.zh: 延壽代蕭望之為左馮翊，而望之遷御史大夫。

translations.literal: Yanshou replaced Xiao Wang-zhi as Left Governor of Pingyi while Wang-zhi was promoted imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiao Wang-zhi rose to imperial counsellor, Yanshou took his old seat at Left Pingyi.

sentences.zh: 侍謁者福為望之道延壽在東郡時放散官錢千餘萬。

translations.literal: The attendant Ye Fu told Wang-zhi that while Yanshou was in the Eastern Commandery he had disbursed over ten million cash of official funds.
translations.idiomatic: A tipster told Wang-zhi that Yanshou had embezzled millions in the Eastern Commandery.

sentences.zh: 望之與丞相丙吉議，吉以為更大赦，不須考。

translations.literal: Wang-zhi discussed it with Chancellor Bing Ji; Ji thought that because of repeated great amnesties there was no need to investigate.
translations.idiomatic: Wang-zhi asked Bing Ji, who said amnesties had wiped the slate.

sentences.zh: 會御史當問事東郡，望之因令并問之。

translations.literal: It happened that an imperial counsellor was due to question affairs in the Eastern Commandery; Wang-zhi therefore ordered him to inquire into this as well.
translations.idiomatic: Wang-zhi told the touring censor to add embezzlement to his brief.

sentences.zh: 延壽聞知，即部吏案校望之在馮翊時廩犧官錢放散百餘萬。

translations.literal: When Yanshou learned of it, he immediately assigned clerks to audit the more than one million cash of granary-sacrifice funds Wang-zhi had disbursed while governor of Pingyi.
translations.idiomatic: Yanshou struck back by auditing Wang-zhi's own grain-fund accounts at Pingyi.

sentences.zh: 廩犧吏掠治急，自引與望之為姦。

translations.literal: The granary clerks were pressed hard under torture and confessed themselves in league with Wang-zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Under the rod the accountants confessed to splitting the loot with Wang-zhi.

sentences.zh: 延壽劾奏，移殿門禁止望之。

translations.literal: Yanshou memorialized impeachment and had Wang-zhi barred at the palace gate.
translations.idiomatic: He filed charges and had guards stop Wang-zhi at the Dadian gate.

sentences.zh: 望之自奏「職在總領天下，聞事不敢不問，而為延壽所拘持。」

translations.literal: Wang-zhi submitted a memorial in his own defense: "My duty is to oversee the empire; hearing of a matter I dare not fail to ask—yet Yanshou has seized and constrained me."
translations.idiomatic: Wang-zhi answered, "My duty is to oversee the empire; hearing of a matter I dare not fail to inquire—yet Yanshou has seized and constrained me."

sentences.zh: 上由是不直延壽，各令窮竟所考。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor thought Yanshou in the wrong and ordered each side to pursue its investigation to the end.
translations.idiomatic: The throne lost patience with Yanshou and told both camps to finish their probes.

sentences.zh: 望之卒無事實，而望之遣御史案東郡，具得其事。

translations.literal: Wang-zhi in the end had no facts to support his charge, yet the imperial counsellor he had sent to investigate the Eastern Commandery obtained the full truth.
translations.idiomatic: Wang-zhi's own case collapsed, but his censor in the Eastern Commandery dug up the dirt Yanshou had buried.

sentences.zh: 延壽在東郡時，試騎士，治飾兵車，畫龍虎朱爵。

translations.literal: While Yanshou was in the Eastern Commandery he reviewed mounted troops, trimmed war chariots, and painted dragons, tigers, and red sparrows on them.
translations.idiomatic: In the Eastern Commandery he staged a full dress review: chariots emblazoned with dragons, tigers, and vermilion finches.

sentences.zh: 延壽衣黃紈方領，駕四馬，傅總，建幢棨，植羽葆，鼓車歌車。

translations.literal: Yanshou wore yellow damask with a square collar, drove four horses with gathered reins, raised signal banners and halberd-stands, planted feathered canopies, and had drum carts and singing carts.
translations.idiomatic: He rode in yellow silk, four-in-hand, with royal-style banners, feather parasols, and a traveling band.

sentences.zh: 功曹引車，皆駕四馬，載棨戟。

translations.literal: The merit assessor led the carriage train; all teams were four-horse and bore halberd racks.
translations.idiomatic: Even his chief clerk's chariot ran four horses and carried a rack of halberds.

sentences.zh: 五騎為伍，分左右部，軍假司馬、千人持幢旁轂。

translations.literal: Five horsemen formed a squad; they split into left and right divisions; acting army majors and chiliarchs held pennants beside the wheel hubs.
translations.idiomatic: Cavalry formed fives, split into wings, with temporary majors and chiliarchs flanking the axles with pennants.

sentences.zh: 歌者先居射室，望見延壽車，噭咷楚歌。

translations.literal: Singers waited first in the archery hall; when they saw Yanshou's carriage they burst into Chu songs.
translations.idiomatic: Chorus girls in the butts wailed Chu airs as his train approached.

sentences.zh: 延壽坐射室，騎吏持戟夾陛列立，騎士從者帶弓鞬羅後。

translations.literal: Yanshou sat in the archery hall; mounted clerks with halberds lined the steps; mounted attendants behind him wore quivers slung at the hip.
translations.idiomatic: He presided from the gallery while guards bracketed the stairs and escorts bristled with bows.

sentences.zh: 令騎士兵車四面營陳，被甲鞮鞪居馬上，抱弩負籣。

translations.literal: He ordered mounted soldiers to array war chariots on four sides, armored and booted in the saddle, hugging crossbows and carrying bolt-boxes.
translations.idiomatic: Armored riders circled the field on horseback, crossbows loaded.

sentences.zh: 又使騎士戲車弄馬盜驂。

translations.literal: He also had horsemen perform chariot tricks, horse stunts, and stealing the outer reins.
translations.idiomatic: The show included circus riding—vaulting, swapping mounts mid-gallop.

sentences.zh: 延壽又取官銅物，候月蝕鑄作刀劍鉤鐔，放效尚方事。

translations.literal: Yanshou further took official bronze, waited for a lunar eclipse, and cast swords, knives, hooks, and buckles, imitating the work of the Imperial Workshop.
translations.idiomatic: He melted government bronze on eclipse nights to forge blades aping the palace armory.

sentences.zh: 及取官錢帛，私假繇使吏。

translations.literal: He also took official cash and silks and lent them out, hiring clerks for private corvée.
translations.idiomatic: He diverted treasury coin and cloth to pay unofficial labor.

sentences.zh: 及治飾車甲三百萬以上。

translations.literal: The cost of trimming chariots and armor exceeded three million cash.
translations.idiomatic: Ornamenting the chariots and armor ran past three million cash.

sentences.zh: 於是望之劾奏延壽上僭不道，又自陳：「前為延壽所奏，今復舉延壽罪，眾庶皆以臣懷不正之心，侵冤延壽。

translations.literal: Thereupon Wang-zhi memorialized impeachment, charging Yanshou with usurping superior ritual and lacking the Way, and himself stated: "Earlier Yanshou impeached me; now I again bring his crimes—the multitude will think I harbor an unjust mind and wrong Yanshou.
translations.idiomatic: Wang-zhi impeached Yanshou for usurping ritual and lacking the Way, then added, "Earlier Yanshou impeached me; now I bring his crimes—the crowd will think I nurse an unjust mind and wrong Yanshou."

sentences.zh: 願下丞相、中二千石、博士議其罪。」

translations.literal: I beg that the matter be sent to the chancellor, ministers at two thousand piculs, and erudites to debate his guilt."
translations.idiomatic: "I beg that the matter be sent to the chancellor, ministers at two thousand piculs, and erudites to debate his guilt."

sentences.zh: 事下公卿，皆以延壽前既無狀，後復誣愬典法大臣，欲以解罪，狡猾不道。

translations.literal: The case went to the high ministers; all held that Yanshou had long behaved without restraint and later slandered the minister who upheld the law, seeking to escape guilt—crafty and lacking the Way.
translations.idiomatic: The consensus: Yanshou had flouted rank for years, then framed a chief minister to save himself.

sentences.zh: 天子惡之，延壽竟坐棄市。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven detested this; Yanshou was sentenced to execution in the market.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved; Yanshou went to the block.

sentences.zh: 吏民數千人送至渭城，老小扶持車轂，爭奏酒炙。

translations.literal: Several thousand clerks and commoners escorted him to Weicheng; old and young steadied his wheel hubs and vied to offer wine and roast meat.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands walked him to Weicheng, clutching his axles and pressing cups and skewers on him.

sentences.zh: 延壽不忍距逆，人人為飲，計飲酒石餘。

translations.literal: Yanshou could not bear to refuse; he drank with each until he had downed more than ten shi of wine.
translations.idiomatic: He drank with everyone until he had taken more than ten stone of liquor.

sentences.zh: 使掾史分謝送者：「遠苦吏民，延壽死無所恨。」

translations.literal: He had his clerks thank the escort: "You have troubled yourselves, clerks and people—Yanshou dies without regret."
translations.idiomatic: Through his clerks he thanked the escort: "You have troubled yourselves, clerks and people—Yanshou dies without regret."

sentences.zh: 百姓莫不流涕。

translations.literal: Not a commoner failed to weep.
translations.idiomatic: The road was wet with tears.

sentences.zh: 延壽三子皆為郎吏。

translations.literal: All three of Yanshou's sons held posts as gentlemen clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Each of his three boys served as a court gentleman.

sentences.zh: 且死，屬其子勿為吏，以己為戒。

translations.literal: As he was about to die he charged his sons never to take office and to take his fate as a warning.
translations.idiomatic: On the scaffold he made them swear off government service.

sentences.zh: 子皆以父言去官不仕。

translations.literal: The sons all left office in obedience to their father's words and would not serve.
translations.idiomatic: They quit their posts at once.

sentences.zh: 至孫威，乃復為吏至將軍。

translations.literal: Not until his grandson Wei did someone again become an official, rising to general.
translations.idiomatic: Only the grandson Han Wei returned to the rolls—and reached general's rank.

sentences.zh: 威亦多恩信，能拊眾，得士死力。

translations.literal: Wei too was much trusted and kind, able to soothe the masses and win soldiers' utmost effort.
translations.idiomatic: Wei inherited the family's gift for winning hearts on the frontier.

sentences.zh: 威又坐奢僭誅，延壽之風類也。

translations.literal: Wei was also executed for extravagance and usurpation of ritual—much in Yanshou's style.
translations.idiomatic: He too died for the same flash and overreach that had killed his grandfather.

sentences.zh: 張敞字子高，本河東平陽人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Chang, courtesy name Zigao, was originally from Pingyang in Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang, styled Zigao, came from Pingyang in Hedong.

sentences.zh: 祖父孺為上谷太守，徙茂陵。

translations.literal: His grandfather Ru had been governor of Shanggu and moved the family to Maoling.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Zhang Ru governed Shanggu before resettling at Maoling.

sentences.zh: 敞父福事孝武帝，官至光祿大夫。

translations.literal: Chang's father Fu had served Emperor Wu and reached grand coachman of the imperial household.
translations.idiomatic: His father Zhang Fu rose to chamberlain for the palace attendants under Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 敞後隨宣帝徙杜陵。

translations.literal: Chang later followed Emperor Xuan in moving the household to Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang followed Emperor Xuan to the new mausoleum town at Duling.

sentences.zh: 敞本以鄉有秩補太守卒史，察廉為甘泉倉長，稍遷太僕丞，杜延年甚奇之。

translations.literal: Chang had originally filled a commandery headman's post, then been made chief clerk to the governor; recommended as incorrupt he became overseer of the Ganquan granary, was gradually promoted to assistant to the chamberlain for the imperial stud, and Du Yannian thought him extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: He worked up from village head to governor's chief clerk, then granary chief at Ganquan, then aide to the chamberlain for the imperial stud—Du Yannian marked him early.

sentences.zh: 會昌邑王徵即位，動作不由法度，敞上書諫曰：「孝昭皇帝蚤崩無嗣，大臣憂懼，選賢聖承宗廟，東迎之日，唯恐屬車之行遲。

translations.literal: When the King of Changyi was summoned to the throne and acted without regard to law, Chang submitted a memorial remonstrating: "Emperor Zhao died young without an heir; the great ministers were anxious and chose a worthy sage to continue the ancestral temple; on the day of the eastern welcome they feared only that the rear carriages would be slow.
translations.idiomatic: When the King of Changyi took the throne and flouted the law, Zhang Chang wrote, "Emperor Zhao died young without an heir; the great ministers were frantic and chose a worthy sage to continue the temple line; on the day of the eastern welcome they feared only that the rear carriages would be slow."

sentences.zh: 今天子以盛年初即位，天下莫不拭目傾耳，觀化聽風。

translations.literal: Now the Son of Heaven has just ascended in the prime of life; all under heaven wipe their eyes and strain their ears to watch the transformation and listen for the wind of policy.
translations.idiomatic: The realm now watched a young emperor to learn whether his court would steady the age or squander it.

sentences.zh: 國輔大臣未褒，而昌邑小輦先遷，此過之大者也。」

translations.literal: The ministers who support the state have not yet been rewarded, yet the minor carriages of Changyi are promoted first—this is a very great fault."
translations.idiomatic: State ministers go unrewarded while Changyi's petty favorites leap ahead—that is the gravest error."

sentences.zh: 後十餘日王賀廢，敞以切諫顯名，擢為豫州刺史。

translations.literal: After more than ten days the king Liu He was deposed; Chang became famous for blunt remonstrance and was promoted to governor of Yuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Within two weeks the prince was thrown out; Zhang Chang's memorial made his name and won him Yuzhou.

sentences.zh: 以數上事有忠言，宣帝徵敞為太中大夫，與于定國並平尚書事。

translations.literal: Because he repeatedly submitted memorials with loyal words, Emperor Xuan summoned Chang as grandee of the palace and had him settle memorials from the masters of writing together with Yu Dingguo.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan called him to court as grandee of the palace to screen palace memorials beside Yu Dingguo.

sentences.zh: 以正違忤大將軍霍光，而使主兵車出軍省減用度，復出為函谷關都尉。

translations.literal: Because he was upright and offended Grand General Huo Guang, he was put in charge of war chariots to lead troops out and cut expenses, then again sent out as commandant of the Hangu Pass.
translations.idiomatic: His bluntness crossed Huo Guang, who shunted him to logistics and then to the Hangu Pass garrison.

sentences.zh: 宣帝初即位，廢王賀在昌邑，上心憚之，徙敞為山陽太守。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan first took the throne the deposed king Liu He remained at Changyi; the emperor was wary of him and transferred Chang to governor of Shanyang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor still feared the deposed Prince He at Changyi, so he parked Zhang Chang in Shanyang as governor.

sentences.zh: 久之，勃海、膠東盜賊並起，敞上書自請治之，曰：「臣聞忠孝之道，退家則盡心於親，進宦則竭力於君。

translations.literal: After a long while bandits rose together in Bohai and Jiaodong; Chang submitted a memorial volunteering to handle it, saying: "I have heard that the way of loyalty and filial piety is, when retired at home, to exhaust one's heart for parents, and when advancing in office, to exhaust one's strength for the ruler.
translations.idiomatic: When revolt broke out in Bohai and Jiaodong, Zhang Chang volunteered, "I have heard that the way of loyalty and filial piety is, at home, to exhaust one's heart for one's parents, and in office, to exhaust one's strength for one's ruler."

sentences.zh: 夫小國中君猶有奮不顧身之臣，況於明天子乎！

translations.literal: Even in a small state a middling ruler still has ministers who rush on without regard for their persons—how much more under an enlightened Son of Heaven!
translations.idiomatic: Even a petty state can breed ministers who die for their lord; how much more should the Han, under a sage Son of Heaven, demand the same.

sentences.zh: 今陛下遊意於太平，勞精於政事，亹亹不舍晝夜。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty's mind roams in quest of great peace and Your essence labors at government, tirelessly day and night.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty toils day and night toward the age of peace.

sentences.zh: 群臣有司宜各竭力致身。

translations.literal: The ministers and responsible officials ought each to exhaust their strength and devote their persons.
translations.idiomatic: Every minister should spend body and soul in reply.

sentences.zh: 山陽郡戶九萬三千，口五十萬以上，訖計盜賊未得者七十七人，它課諸事亦略如此。

translations.literal: Shanyang commandery has ninety-three thousand households and more than five hundred thousand persons; down to the present seventy-seven thieves remain uncaptured; other registered targets are roughly similar.
translations.idiomatic: Shanyang counts ninety-three thousand households and half a million mouths, yet seventy-seven named bandits still roam free—other quotas look the same.

sentences.zh: 臣敞愚駑，既無以佐思慮，久處閒郡，身逸樂而忘國事，非忠孝之節也。

translations.literal: Your subject Chang is dull; he has been unable to aid Your deliberations and has long stayed in an idle commandery, his body at ease and pleased while forgetting state affairs—this is not the standard of loyalty and filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: I have warmed a quiet seat while bandits multiply—that is neither loyal nor filial.

sentences.zh: 伏聞膠東、勃海左右郡歲數不登，盜賊並起，至攻官寺，篡囚徒，搜市朝，劫列侯。

translations.literal: I have heard that Jiaodong and Bohai and neighboring commanderies have suffered poor harvests for years, bandits rising together until they attack yamen, break out prisoners, ransack markets, and rob marquises.
translations.idiomatic: I hear the eastern coast has failed several harvests; mobs sack offices, free convicts, loot the markets, and even rob marquises.

sentences.zh: 吏失綱紀，姦軌不禁。

translations.literal: Clerks have lost the guiding cords and wicked paths are unchecked.
translations.idiomatic: Local government has lost its grip.

sentences.zh: 臣敞不敢愛身避死，唯明詔之所處，願盡力摧挫其暴虐，存撫其孤弱。

translations.literal: Your subject Chang dares not spare his body or flee death; wherever Your clear edict assigns him, he begs to exhaust his strength in crushing their violence and cherishing the orphaned and weak.
translations.idiomatic: Send me where you will—I will break the gangs and shelter the helpless.

sentences.zh: 事即有業，所至郡條奏其所由廢及所以興之狀。」
