translations.literal: If the task achieves results, at each commandery he reaches he will memorialize item by item the reasons for decline and the means by which order was restored."
translations.idiomatic: If I succeed, I will report from each county how rot set in and how I set it right."

sentences.zh: 書奏，天子徵敞，拜膠東相，賜黃金三十斤。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the emperor summoned Chang, appointed him chancellor of Jiaodong, and granted thirty jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named him chancellor of Jiaodong and weighed out thirty pounds of gold.

sentences.zh: 敞辭之官，自請治劇郡非賞罰無以勸善懲惡，吏追捕有功效者，願得壹切比三輔尤異。

translations.literal: When Chang took leave to go to his post he requested that in governing a difficult commandery nothing but rewards and punishments can encourage good and punish evil, and that clerks whose pursuit of bandits proved effective might receive blanket authority comparable to the exceptional powers of the Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: On departure he asked for the emergency powers the capital prefects enjoyed—bounties, summary justice, and promotions for good arrests.

sentences.zh: 天子許之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven granted it.
translations.idiomatic: The throne agreed.

sentences.zh: 敞到膠東，明設購賞，開群盜令相捕斬除罪。

translations.literal: When Chang reached Jiaodong he openly posted rewards and issued an order allowing bandit gangs to capture and behead one another in exchange for remission of guilt.
translations.idiomatic: He posted bounties and offered amnesty to any gang that delivered another gang's heads.

sentences.zh: 吏追捕有功，上名尚書調補縣令者數十人。

translations.literal: Clerks who succeeded in pursuit had their names sent to the masters of writing and several tens were transferred to fill county magistracies.
translations.idiomatic: Successful officers went to the capital personnel office and dozens jumped straight to magistrate.

sentences.zh: 由是盜賊解散，傳相捕斬。

translations.literal: Thereupon the bandits scattered, handing one another over for capture and execution.
translations.idiomatic: The gangs turned on one another for the price on their heads.

sentences.zh: 吏民歙然，國中遂平。

translations.literal: Clerks and people were awed into silence; the kingdom was pacified.
translations.idiomatic: The realm went quiet; the rebellion melted.

sentences.zh: 居頃之，王太后數出游獵，敞奏書諫曰：「臣聞秦王好淫聲，葉陽后為不聽鄭衛之樂；

translations.literal: After a time the king's mother, the queen dowager, repeatedly went out hunting; Chang submitted a memorial remonstrating: "I have heard that the King of Qin loved dissolute music and Queen Yeyang therefore refused to listen to Zheng and Wei tunes;
translations.idiomatic: When the dowager of Jiaodong took to the hunt, Zhang Chang wrote, "I have heard that the King of Qin loved dissolute music and Queen Yeyang therefore refused to listen to Zheng and Wei airs."

sentences.zh: 楚嚴好田獵，樊姬為之不食鳥獸之肉。

translations.literal: King Yan of Chu loved the hunt, and Lady Fan Ji for his sake would not eat the flesh of birds or beasts.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Fan Ji of Chu gave up fowl and flesh until her king abandoned his obsession with the hunt.

sentences.zh: 口非惡旨甘，耳非憎絲竹也，所以抑心意，絕耆欲者，將以率二君而全宗祀也。

translations.literal: It was not that their mouths hated fine flavors or their ears hated strings and pipes; they suppressed their hearts and cut off excessive desires in order to lead their two lords and preserve the ancestral sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: They starved the senses to steer their lords back toward the altars.

sentences.zh: 禮，君母出門則乘輜軿，下堂則從傅母，進退則鳴玉佩，內飾則結綢繆。

translations.literal: By ritual, when the ruler's mother leaves the gate she rides in a curtained carriage; when she descends the hall she follows her tutor nurse; advancing and retreating she makes her jade pendants chime; indoors her ornaments are properly tied.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual says a queen mother rides veiled, walks behind a nurse, jingles with each step, and dresses with every ribbon in place.

sentences.zh: 此言尊貴所以自斂制，不從恣之義也。

translations.literal: This speaks of how honor and nobility restrain themselves and do not follow wantonness.
translations.idiomatic: That is how dignity disciplines appetite.

sentences.zh: 今太后資質淑美，慈愛寬仁，諸侯莫不聞，而少以田獵縱欲為名，於以上聞，亦未宜也。

translations.literal: Now the dowager's endowment is virtuous and beautiful, kind, loving, and magnanimous—no feudal lord has failed to hear of it—yet if she gains a reputation for slight indulgence in hunting and yielding to desire, it is also not fitting that this reach superior ears.
translations.idiomatic: Your mother is praised for grace; a name for pleasure hunts would ill become the court's ear.

sentences.zh: 唯觀覽於往古，全行乎來今，令后姬得有所法則，下臣有所稱誦，臣敞幸甚！」

translations.literal: Only look to antiquity and perfect your conduct for the present, so palace ladies may have a model and lower ministers something to praise—then your subject Chang will be most fortunate!"
translations.idiomatic: Look backward to the sages, forward to a flawless example for the harem and a story officials can tell with pride."

sentences.zh: 書奏，太后止不復出。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the dowager ceased going out.
translations.idiomatic: She never hunted again.

sentences.zh: 是時潁川太守黃霸以治行第一入守京兆尹。

translations.literal: At that time the governor of Yingchuan, Huang Ba, by top administrative record entered as acting metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Ba of Yingchuan, first on the merit list, took acting charge of the capital.

sentences.zh: 霸視事數月，不稱，罷歸潁川。

translations.literal: After several months in office Ba did not fit the post and was dismissed back to Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Within months he washed out and went home to Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 於是制詔御史：「其以膠東相敞守京兆尹。」

translations.literal: Thereupon an edict ordered the imperial secretary: "Let the chancellor of Jiaodong, Chang, act as metropolitan Governor."
translations.idiomatic: An edict ordered the imperial secretary: "Let Chancellor Zhang Chang of Jiaodong act as metropolitan Governor."

sentences.zh: 自趙廣漢誅後，比更守尹，如霸等數人，皆不稱職。

translations.literal: Since Zhao Guanghan's execution, successive acting and confirmed governors—several men like Ba—had none proved competent.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhao Guanghan's death a string of appointees, Huang Ba among them, had failed the capital.

sentences.zh: 京師寖廢，長安市偷盜尤多，百賈苦之。

translations.literal: The capital region declined; theft in the Chang'an market was especially heavy and merchants suffered.
translations.idiomatic: Chang'an markets slid toward anarchy; the guilds begged for relief.

sentences.zh: 上以問敞，敞以為可禁。

translations.literal: The emperor asked Chang, who thought it could be stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor turned to Zhang Chang, who said he could fix it.

sentences.zh: 敞既視事，求問長安父老，偷盜酋長數人，居皆溫厚，出從童騎，閭里以為長者。

translations.literal: Once Chang took office he questioned Chang'an elders and identified several chiefs of thieves—men who at home were mild and warm, went abroad with boy attendants, and were thought elders of their lanes.
translations.idiomatic: He found the fence bosses: respectable neighbors who rode out with child pages and looked like grandfathers.

sentences.zh: 敞皆召見責問，因貰其罪，把其宿負，令致諸偷以自贖。

translations.literal: Chang summoned them all, rebuked them, then forgave their crimes, seized their old debts, and ordered them to deliver the thieves in order to redeem themselves.
translations.idiomatic: He called them in, forgave past sins, and traded amnesty for every pickpocket they could deliver.

sentences.zh: 偷長曰：「今一旦召詣府，恐諸偷驚駭，願一切受署。」

translations.literal: The thief chief said: "If we are summoned to the yamen at once, the other thieves will panic; we beg to receive appointment in full."
translations.idiomatic: The thief chief said, "If we are summoned to the yamen at once, the other thieves will panic; we beg to receive commissions first."

sentences.zh: 敞皆以為吏，遣歸休。

translations.literal: Chang appointed them all clerks and sent them home on leave.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang made them deputies and sent them home.

sentences.zh: 置酒，小偷悉來賀，且飲醉，偷長以赭汙其衣裾。

translations.literal: He set out wine; the minor thieves all came to congratulate, drank until drunk, and the thief chief smeared the hems of their robes with ochre.
translations.idiomatic: He threw a banquet; when the small fry were drunk, their leader dabbed ochre on every coat tail.

sentences.zh: 吏坐里閭閱出者，汙赭輒收縛之，一日捕得數百人。

translations.literal: Clerks sat at lane gates inspecting those who left; whoever bore ochre stains was seized and bound—in one day several hundred were taken.
translations.idiomatic: Guards at every alley mouth grabbed anyone with a rust-colored hem—hundreds in a day.

sentences.zh: 窮治所犯，或一人百餘發，盡行法罰。

translations.literal: They were exhaustively prosecuted; some had committed more than a hundred offenses and all were punished according to law.
translations.idiomatic: Follow-up trials showed career criminals with a hundred counts each.

sentences.zh: 由是枹鼓稀鳴，市無偷盜，天子嘉之。

translations.literal: Thereafter drum-beats for theft were rarely heard; the market had no thieves; the Son of Heaven praised him.
translations.idiomatic: The night-watch drums fell silent; the emperor applauded.

sentences.zh: 敞為人敏疾，賞罰分明，見惡輒取，時時越法縱舍，有足大者。

translations.literal: Chang was by nature quick and sharp, clear in rewards and punishments; when he saw evil he seized it, yet from time to time overstepped the law in leniency or release—faults large enough to notice.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang was brilliant and decisive, sometimes too quick with mercy or the rod.

sentences.zh: 其治京兆，略循趙廣漢之跡。

translations.literal: In governing the capital he broadly followed Zhao Guanghan's tracks.
translations.idiomatic: He ran the capital much as Zhao Guanghan had.

sentences.zh: 方略耳目，發伏禁姦，不如廣漢，然敞本治春秋，以經術自輔，其政頗雜儒雅，往往表賢顯善，不醇用誅罰，以此能自全，竟免於刑戮。

translations.literal: In strategy, informers, exposing the hidden, and forbidding wickedness he did not match Guanghan, yet Chang had originally studied the Spring and Autumn Annals and used classical learning to aid himself; his government mixed in Confucian polish, often commending the worthy and displaying the good, not relying purely on executions and fines—thus he could preserve himself to the end and escaped execution.
translations.idiomatic: He lacked Zhao's spy web but leavened police work with Confucian praise and blame—and lived to tell the tale.

sentences.zh: 京兆典京師，長安中浩穰，於三輔尤為劇。

translations.literal: The metropolitan prefecture administers the capital; Chang'an is crowded and chaotic, the most difficult of the Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: The capital prefecture is the empire's cockpit; Chang'an is the hardest post of the three.

sentences.zh: 郡國二千石以高弟入守，及為真，久者不過二三年，近者數月一歲，輒毀傷失名，以罪過罷。

translations.literal: Chiefs at two thousand piculs who enter on top rank to act and then are confirmed last at most two or three years, the near ones only several months or a year, then are ruined and lose reputation, dismissed for crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Most governors lasted a year or two, some only months, before scandal ended them.

sentences.zh: 唯廣漢及敞為久任職。

translations.literal: Only Guanghan and Chang held office for long.
translations.idiomatic: Only Zhao Guanghan and Zhang Chang endured.

sentences.zh: 敞為京兆，朝廷每有大議，引古今，處便宜，公卿皆服，天子數從之。

translations.literal: When Chang was metropolitan Governor the court had great debates; citing antiquity and modern times he settled what was advantageous; the high ministers submitted and the Son of Heaven repeatedly followed him.
translations.idiomatic: In great council he quoted classics and carried the day so often the emperor habitually took his side.

sentences.zh: 然敞無威儀，時罷朝會，過走馬章臺街，使御吏驅，自以便面拊馬。

translations.literal: Yet Chang had no dignified bearing; sometimes after court dismissal he galloped through Zhangtai Street, had his charioteer whip the horses, and himself fanned the team with a screen.
translations.idiomatic: Off duty he raced down Zhangtai Street fanning his ponies like a boy racer.

sentences.zh: 又為婦畫眉，長安中傳張京兆眉憮。

translations.literal: He also painted his wife's eyebrows; in Chang'an they spread the tale of Metropolitan Zhang's wandering brows.
translations.idiomatic: He sketched his wife's eyebrows; the capital mocked "Governor Zhang's bedroom calligraphy."

sentences.zh: 有司以奏敞。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized Chang.
translations.idiomatic: The censorate reported him.

sentences.zh: 上問之，對曰：「臣聞閨房之內，夫婦之私，有過於畫眉者。」

translations.literal: When the emperor asked, he replied: "I have heard that within the women's quarters, between husband and wife, there are private acts beyond painting eyebrows."
translations.idiomatic: Before the emperor he replied, "I have heard that within the women's quarters, between husband and wife, there are private acts more intimate than painting eyebrows."

sentences.zh: 上愛其能，弗備責也。

translations.literal: The emperor loved his ability and did not fully blame him.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan laughed off the charge.

sentences.zh: 然終不得大位。

translations.literal: Yet he never reached the highest offices.
translations.idiomatic: He still never rose to the summit.

sentences.zh: 敞與蕭望之、于定國相善。

translations.literal: Chang was on good terms with Xiao Wang-zhi and Yu Dingguo.
translations.idiomatic: He was friends with Xiao Wang-zhi and Yu Dingguo.

sentences.zh: 始敞與定國俱以諫昌邑王超遷。

translations.literal: At first Chang and Dingguo had both been abruptly promoted for remonstrating the King of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: He and Yu Dingguo had vaulted the ranks together on the Changyi memorials.

sentences.zh: 定國為大夫平尚書事，敞出為刺史，時望之為大行丞。

translations.literal: Dingguo became a grandee settling masters-of-writing business while Chang went out as governor; at that time Wang-zhi was assistant to the chamberlain for dependencies.
translations.idiomatic: Yu stayed at court on documents; Zhang went to the provinces while Xiao was still a foreign-affairs aide.

sentences.zh: 後望之先至御史大夫，定國後至丞相，敞終不過郡守。

translations.literal: Later Wang-zhi first reached imperial counsellor, Dingguo later reached chancellor, while Chang never rose past governor.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao became censor-in-chief, Yu became chancellor; Zhang never left the commandery level.

sentences.zh: 為京兆九歲，坐與光祿勳楊惲厚善，後惲坐大逆誅，公卿奏惲黨友，不宜處位，等比皆免，而敞奏獨寢不下。

translations.literal: After nine years as metropolitan Governor he was implicated for close friendship with Yang Yun, chamberlain for the palace garrison; later Yun was executed for great treason, and the high ministers memorialized that Yun's associates should not hold office—all were dismissed in like degree—yet the memorial concerning Chang alone lay dormant and was not issued.
translations.idiomatic: Nine years in the capital ended when his friend Yang Yun fell for lese-majeste; the court cashiered Yun's whole circle, but Zhang Chang's dismissal slip somehow never left the desk.

sentences.zh: 敞使卒捕掾絮舜有所案驗。

translations.literal: Chang had runners arrest the clerk-investigator Xu Shun on some matter under review.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered bailiffs to seize his investigator Xu Shun.

sentences.zh: 舜以敞劾奏當免，不肯為敞竟事，私歸其家。

translations.literal: Shun thought Chang's impeachment would certainly mean dismissal and refused to finish Chang's business, going home on his own.
translations.idiomatic: Xu assumed Zhang was finished and walked off the job.

sentences.zh: 人或諫舜，舜曰：「吾為是公盡力多矣，今五日京兆耳，安能復案事？」

translations.literal: Someone remonstrated with Shun; Shun said: "I have already exhausted my strength for this lord; now he is a metropolitan Governor for five days—how can he again handle cases?"
translations.idiomatic: When scolded, Xu sneered that Zhang was a "five-day governor" with no time left to settle dockets.

sentences.zh: 敞聞舜語，即部吏收舜繫獄。

translations.literal: When Chang heard Shun's words he immediately assigned clerks to seize Shun and bind him in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang had him dragged to jail at once.

sentences.zh: 是時冬月未盡數日，案事吏晝夜驗治舜，竟致其死事。

translations.literal: It was then winter, with only a few days left in the month; the clerks handling the case examined and tortured Shun day and night until they had built a capital case.
translations.idiomatic: In the last days of winter his jailers worked Xu Shun round the clock until they had a hanging charge.

sentences.zh: 舜當出死，敞使主簿持教告舜曰：「五日京兆竟何如？

translations.literal: When Shun was due to go out to execution, Chang had the chief clerk bring written instructions telling Shun: "How about that Metropolitan Governor of five days?"
translations.idiomatic: On the eve of execution Zhang sent his registrar with a note: "Still think I'm a five-day governor?"

sentences.zh: 冬月已盡，延命乎？」

translations.literal: "Winter month is ended—did you get a reprieve on your life?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked whether the turn of the month had bought Xu Shun a stay of execution.

sentences.zh: 乃棄舜市。

translations.literal: Thereupon he had Shun exposed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: He had Xu Shun cut down in public.

sentences.zh: 會立春，行冤獄使者出，舜家載尸，并編敞教，自言使者。

translations.literal: It coincided with the Beginning of Spring; the agent reviewing wrongful imprisonment went out; Shun's family loaded the corpse, bound together with Chang's instructions, and appealed in person to the agent.
translations.idiomatic: Spring audits began; Xu's kin hauled the body and Zhang's taunting note to the touring inspector.

sentences.zh: 使者奏敞賊殺不辜。

translations.literal: The agent memorialized that Chang had murdered the innocent with malice.
translations.idiomatic: The inspector reported deliberate murder.

sentences.zh: 天子薄其罪，欲令敞得自便利，即先下敞前坐楊惲不宜處位奏，免為庶人。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven thought his offense slight, wishing to let Chang arrange matters to his own advantage, and first issued the earlier memorial that Chang's standing with Yang Yun made him unfit for office, dismissing him as a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor went easy: he rubber-stamped the old Yang Yun guilt first so Zhang could slip to commoner status instead of the block.

sentences.zh: 敞免奏既下，詣闕上印綬，便從闕下亡命。

translations.literal: When Chang's dismissal was issued, he went to the palace to surrender seals and cords, then fled for his life from beneath the palace gate.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang handed in his seals and vanished from the capital the same hour.

sentences.zh: 數月，京師吏民解弛，枹鼓數起，而冀州部中有大賊。

translations.literal: Several months later, capital clerks and people grew lax; drum-beats for theft sounded repeatedly, and great bandits appeared within the Jizhou inspectorate.
translations.idiomatic: Within months the capital slid back toward chaos and Jizhou boiled with outlaw armies.

sentences.zh: 天子思敞功效，使使者即家在所召敞。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven thought of Chang's achievements and sent an agent to summon him at his home wherever he was found.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent recruiters to Zhang's hideout.

sentences.zh: 敞身被重劾，及使者至，妻子家室皆泣惶懼，而敞獨笑曰：「吾身亡命為民，郡吏當就捕，今使者來，此天子欲用我也。」

translations.literal: Though Chang bore heavy impeachment, when the agent arrived his wife, children, and household all wept in terror, but Chang alone laughed and said: "I am a fugitive commoner; commandery officers should come to arrest me—now an agent has come; this means the Son of Heaven wishes to employ me."
translations.idiomatic: His household wept in terror, but Zhang Chang alone laughed and said, "I am a fugitive commoner—commandery officers should come to arrest me; now an imperial agent has arrived, which means the Son of Heaven means to employ me again."

sentences.zh: 即裝隨使者詣公書上車曰：「臣前幸得備位列卿，待罪京兆，坐殺賊捕掾絮舜。

translations.literal: Thereupon he packed and followed the agent; on the carriage he submitted a memorial saying: "Your subject formerly had the fortune to stand among the ministers, awaiting punishment as metropolitan Governor, for killing the thief-catching clerk Xu Shun.
translations.idiomatic: He packed, followed the agent, and on the road submitted a memorial: "Your subject formerly stood among the ministers, awaiting judgment as metropolitan Governor, for killing the thief-catching clerk Xu Shun."

sentences.zh: 舜本臣敞素所厚吏，數蒙恩貸，以臣有章劾當免，受記考事，便歸臥家，謂臣『五日京兆』，背恩忘義，傷化薄俗。

translations.literal: Shun was originally a clerk whom your subject had long favored, who repeatedly received grace and pardon; because your subject faced memorial impeachment and dismissal, when he received assignment to investigate he went home to lie down, calling your subject 'Metropolitan Governor of five days'—betraying grace, forgetting righteousness, wounding moral transformation and thinning custom.
translations.idiomatic: Xu had been a favored clerk who abandoned an assigned inquest, sneered that Zhang was only a "five-day governor," and threw away every kindness his patron had shown him.

sentences.zh: 臣竊以舜無狀，枉法以誅之。

translations.literal: Your subject privately thought Shun without propriety and executed him by twisting the law.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang admits he stretched the statutes to kill him.

sentences.zh: 臣敞賊殺無辜，鞠獄故不直，雖伏明法，死無所恨。」

translations.literal: Your subject murdered the innocent and deliberately rendered trial unjust; though he submits to the clear law, in death he has no resentment."
translations.idiomatic: He pleads guilty to judicial murder and accepts whatever sentence follows."

sentences.zh: 天子引見敞，拜為冀州刺史。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven led Chang in audience and appointed him Inspector of Jizhou.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received him and named him inspector of Jizhou.

sentences.zh: 敞起亡命，復奉使典州。

translations.literal: Chang rose from fugitive life again to receive commission and govern a province.
translations.idiomatic: From outlaw to provincial governor in one edict.

sentences.zh: 既到部，而廣川王國群輩不道，賊連發，不得。

translations.literal: Once he reached his inspectorate, the disorderly gang of the Prince of Guangchuan's kingdom acted without the Way; thefts linked together and the culprits could not be taken.
translations.idiomatic: Guangchuan's royal clique ran wild and eluded every net.

sentences.zh: 敞以耳目發起賊主名區處，誅其渠帥。

translations.literal: Chang used his informers to expose the bandit leaders' names and hiding places and executed their chiefs.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang's ears named the ringleaders; he beheaded them.

sentences.zh: 廣川王姬昆弟及王同族宗室劉調等通行為之囊橐，吏逐捕窮窘，蹤跡皆入王宮。

translations.literal: The prince's consorts' younger brothers and royal kinsmen such as Liu Tiao acted together as fences; officers pursued them into extremity and all trails led into the royal palace.
translations.idiomatic: Princesses' brothers and Liu Tiao laundered the loot until the trail dead-ended inside the palace walls.

sentences.zh: 敞自將郡國吏，車數百兩，圍守王宮，搜索調等，果得之殿屋重轑中。

translations.literal: Chang personally led commandery and kingdom officers, several hundred carriages, surrounded the royal palace, searched for Tiao and the others, and indeed found them in the layered roof timbers of a hall.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang ringed the palace with hundreds of wagons, tore up the rafters, and dragged Liu Tiao out of the roof.

sentences.zh: 敞傅吏皆捕格斷頭，縣其頭王宮門外。

translations.literal: Chang's attendant officers all captured them in combat, cut off their heads, and suspended the heads outside the royal palace gate.
translations.idiomatic: His men killed them in the fight and nailed the heads to the palace gate.

sentences.zh: 因劾奏廣川王。

translations.literal: Thereupon he impeached the Prince of Guangchuan.
translations.idiomatic: He then indicted the king.

sentences.zh: 天子不忍致法，削其戶。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven could not bear to apply the full penalty and reduced the prince's household allotment.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared execution but slashed the fief.

sentences.zh: 敞居部歲餘，冀州盜賊禁止。

translations.literal: Chang stayed in his inspectorate over a year and banditry in Jizhou was stopped.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Jizhou was quiet.

sentences.zh: 守太原太守，滿歲為真，太原郡清。

translations.literal: He guarded as acting Governor of Taiyuan; after a full year he was confirmed; Taiyuan commandery became clean.
translations.idiomatic: He moved to Taiyuan as acting governor, won confirmation, and left the commandery spotless.

sentences.zh: 頃之，宣帝崩。

translations.literal: Before long Emperor Xuan died.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan died soon after.

sentences.zh: 元帝初即位，待詔鄭朋薦敞先帝名臣，宜傅輔皇太子。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan first ascended, the awaiting-edict Zheng Peng recommended Chang as a famous minister of the late emperor, fit to tutor the crown prince.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan's courtier Zheng Peng urged Zhang Chang to tutor the heir.

sentences.zh: 上以問前將軍蕭望之，望之以為敞能吏，任治煩亂，材輕非師傅之器。

translations.literal: The emperor asked Former General Xiao Wang-zhi; Wang-zhi thought Chang a capable officer for vexed and chaotic districts, but light in weight and not material for a tutor's role.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao Wang-zhi called him a brawler, not a teacher.

sentences.zh: 天子使使者徵敞，欲以為左馮翊。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven sent an agent to summon Chang, intending to appoint him Left Governor of Pingyi.
translations.idiomatic: The throne still meant to give him Left Pingyi.

sentences.zh: 會病卒。

translations.literal: It happened that he died of illness first.
translations.idiomatic: He died of illness before the chariot arrived.

sentences.zh: 敞所誅殺太原吏吏家怨敞，隨至杜陵刺殺敞中子璜。

translations.literal: Families of Taiyuan clerks whom Chang had killed resented Chang; they followed him to Duling and assassinated Chang's middle son Zhang Huang.
translations.idiomatic: Taiyuan families he had ruined trailed him to Duling and murdered his second son.

sentences.zh: 敞三子官皆至都尉。

translations.literal: All three of Chang's sons who held office reached chief commandant rank.
translations.idiomatic: Each of his three boys who served rose to chief commandant.

sentences.zh: 初，敞為京兆尹，而敞弟武拜為梁相。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Chang was metropolitan Governor, his younger brother Wu was appointed chancellor of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: While Zhang Chang ran the capital, his brother Zhang Wu became chancellor of Liang.

sentences.zh: 是時梁王驕貴，民多豪彊，號為難治。

translations.literal: At that time the King of Liang was arrogant as close imperial kin; the people had many powerful houses; the kingdom was called hard to govern.
translations.idiomatic: Liang's king was a spoiled cousin; magnates filled the fields—it was a notorious posting.

sentences.zh: 敞問武：「欲何以治梁？」

translations.literal: Chang asked Wu: "By what means do you intend to govern Liang?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang asked, "By what means do you intend to govern Liang?"

sentences.zh: 武敬憚兄，謙不肯言。

translations.literal: Wu revered and feared his elder brother and modestly refused to speak.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Wu would not say.

sentences.zh: 敞使吏送至關，戒吏自問武。

translations.literal: Chang had an officer escort him to the pass and instructed the officer to question Wu himself.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang sent a clerk to the border to worm the answer out of him.

sentences.zh: 武應曰：「馭黠馬者利其銜策，梁國大都，吏民凋敝，且當以柱後惠文彈治之耳。」

translations.literal: Wu replied: "One who drives a crafty horse profits from bit and whip; Liang is a great metropolis, its clerks and people exhausted—one must simply govern it with the 'post-pillar Huiwen' cap."
translations.idiomatic: "Treat Liang like a rank stallion," Wu said—"bit, whip, and the black cap of a Qin jailer."

sentences.zh: 秦時獄法吏冠柱後惠文，武意欲以刑法治梁。

translations.literal: In Qin times law clerks wore the post-pillar Huiwen cap; Wu's meaning was to govern Liang with penal law.
translations.idiomatic: He meant hard-line criminal justice, the way Qin clerks had run trials.

sentences.zh: 吏還道之，敞笑曰：「審如掾言，武必辨治梁矣。」

translations.literal: When the officer returned and reported it, Chang laughed and said: "If it is truly as the clerk said, Wu will certainly be able to govern Liang well."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang laughed: "Then my brother will handle Liang."

sentences.zh: 武既到官，其治有跡，亦能吏也。

translations.literal: Once Wu reached his post, his administration showed results; he too was a capable officer.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Wu proved a strong governor in his own right.

sentences.zh: 敞孫竦，王莽時至郡守，封侯，博學文雅過於敞，然政事不及也。

translations.literal: Chang's grandson Song, under Wang Mang, reached governor and marquis; he was more learned and refined than Chang, yet inferior in administration.
translations.idiomatic: His grandson Zhang Song, under Wang Mang, rose higher in scholarship but never matched him as an executive.

sentences.zh: 竦死，敞無後。

translations.literal: When Song died, Chang had no posterity.
translations.idiomatic: Song's line ended Zhang Chang's descendants.

sentences.zh: 王尊字子贛，涿郡高陽人也。

translations.literal: Wang Zun, courtesy name Zigong, was from Gaoyang in Zhuo Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun, styled Zigong, came from Gaoyang in Zhuo.

sentences.zh: 少孤，歸諸父，使牧羊澤中。

translations.literal: Orphaned young, he went to his uncles and was set to herd sheep in the marsh.
translations.idiomatic: His uncles put him to minding sheep on the marsh.

sentences.zh: 尊竊學問，能史書。

translations.literal: Zun secretly pursued learning and could write clerical script.
translations.idiomatic: He stole lessons and learned clerical hand.

sentences.zh: 年十三，求為獄小吏。

translations.literal: At age thirteen he sought appointment as a minor jail clerk.
translations.idiomatic: At thirteen he begged for a jailer's stool.

sentences.zh: 數歲，給事太守府，問詔書行事，尊無不對。

translations.literal: After several years he served in the governor's mansion; when asked about edict precedents, Zun had an answer for every question.
translations.idiomatic: In the governor's yamen he could quote any edict from memory.

sentences.zh: 太守奇之，除補書佐，署守屬監獄。

translations.literal: The governor marveled at him, appointed him clerk-scribe, and assigned him as acting attendant supervising prisons.
translations.idiomatic: The governor made him a writing clerk and acting jail overseer.

sentences.zh: 久之，尊稱病去，事師郡文學官，治尚書、論語，略通大義。

translations.literal: After a long while Zun pleaded illness and left, studied under the commandery erudite, worked on the Documents and the Analects, and broadly grasped their main meaning.
translations.idiomatic: He quit to study the Documents and Analects under the commandery scholar.

sentences.zh: 復召署守屬治獄，為郡決曹史。

translations.literal: He was again summoned as acting attendant for trials and became the commandery's clerk of capital decisions.
translations.idiomatic: He returned as capital-case clerk for the commandery.

sentences.zh: 數歲，以令舉幽州刺史從事。

translations.literal: After several years, on statutory recommendation he was promoted to staff officer on the Inspector of Youzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Statute carried him to a staff post under the inspector of Youzhou.

sentences.zh: 而太守察尊廉，補遼西鹽官長。

translations.literal: The governor, finding Zun incorrupt, appointed him overseer of the western Liao salt office.
translations.idiomatic: The governor named him head of the Liao salt monopoly for incorruptibility.

sentences.zh: 數上書言便宜事，事下丞相御史。

translations.literal: He repeatedly submitted memorials on advantageous measures; the matters went down to the chancellor and imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: He peppered the capital with policy memos that reached the chancellor's desk.

sentences.zh: 初元中，舉直言，遷虢令，轉守槐里，兼行美陽令事。

translations.literal: In the Chuyuan era he was recommended for blunt speech, promoted to magistrate of Guo, transferred to acting charge of Huaili while concurrently handling Meiyang.
translations.idiomatic: On a "blunt counsel" nomination he became magistrate of Guo, then acting Huaili with Meiyang added.

sentences.zh: 春正月，美陽女子告假子不孝，曰：「兒常以我為妻，妒笞我。」

translations.literal: In spring, first month, a woman of Meiyang reported her foster son for unfilial conduct, saying: "The boy constantly treats me as his wife, is jealous, and flogs me."
translations.idiomatic: A woman of Meiyang reported her foster son, saying, "The boy treats me as his wife, is jealous, and beats me."

sentences.zh: 尊聞之，遣吏收捕驗問，辭服。

translations.literal: When Zun heard, he sent officers to arrest and question him; he confessed.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun arrested him; he confessed.

sentences.zh: 尊曰：「律無妻母之法，聖人所不忍書，此經所謂造獄者也。」

translations.literal: Zun said: "The statutes have no law for 'wife-mother'—the sages could not bear to write it; this is what the classics call making a prison case where none should exist."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun said, "The statutes have no crime called 'wife-mother'—the sages could not bear to write it; this is what the classics mean by manufacturing a case where none should exist."

sentences.zh: 尊於是出坐廷上，取不孝子縣磔著樹，使騎吏五人張弓射殺之，吏民驚駭。

translations.literal: Zun thereupon came out and sat in the court, had the unfilial son hauled up and dismembered on a tree, had five mounted clerks draw bows and shoot him to death; clerks and people were stunned.
translations.idiomatic: He dragged the man into the courtyard, tied him to a tree, and had five archers shoot him apart while the crowd watched in horror.

sentences.zh: 後上行幸雍，過虢，尊供張如法而辦。

translations.literal: Later the emperor traveled to Yong, passed Guo, and Zun supplied the progress according to law and handled it well.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor passed through Guo, Wang Zun's reception train was flawless.

sentences.zh: 以高弟擢為安定太守。

translations.literal: By top evaluation he was promoted to governor of Anding.
translations.idiomatic: Top marks won him Anding.

sentences.zh: 到官，出教告屬縣曰：「令長丞尉奉法守城，為民父母，抑彊扶弱，宣恩廣澤，甚勞苦矣。

translations.literal: On reaching his post he issued instructions to the subordinate counties, saying: "Magistrates, chiefs, and captains who uphold the law and guard the walls, acting as fathers and mothers to the people, checking the mighty and aiding the weak, spreading grace and broad moisture, labor bitterly.
translations.idiomatic: On taking office he issued instructions to the counties: "Magistrates, chiefs, and captains who uphold the law and guard the walls, acting as fathers and mothers to the people, checking the mighty and aiding the weak, spreading grace and broad moisture, labor bitterly."

sentences.zh: 太守以今日至府，願諸君卿勉力正身以率下。

translations.literal: The governor arrives at the mansion today; I hope you gentlemen will strive to correct yourselves and lead those below.
translations.idiomatic: "The governor enters the mansion today; I hope you gentlemen will straighten yourselves and lead those below."

sentences.zh: 故行貪鄙，能變更者與為治。

translations.literal: Those who in the past acted greedy and base—whoever can reform may join in good order.
translations.idiomatic: "Those who were greedy and base in the past—whoever can reform may share in good order."

sentences.zh: 明慎所職，毋以身試法。」

translations.literal: Be bright and careful in your duties; do not test the law with your bodies."
translations.idiomatic: "Be bright and careful in your duties; do not test the law with your bodies."

sentences.zh: 又出教敕掾功曹「各自底厲，助太守為治。

translations.literal: He also issued instructions charging the merit assessor: "Each of you must spur yourselves on and help the governor govern.
translations.idiomatic: He also charged his senior staff: "Each of you must spur himself on and help the governor govern."

sentences.zh: 其不中用，趣自避退，毋久妨賢。

translations.literal: Those who are not fit for use should quickly withdraw of themselves and not long obstruct the worthy.
translations.idiomatic: "Those who are not fit should withdraw at once and not long block worthier men."

sentences.zh: 夫羽翮不修，則不可以致千里；

translations.literal: If the wing-feathers are not trimmed, one cannot fly a thousand li;
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb says, a bird with broken feathers cannot fly a thousand li.

sentences.zh: 闑內不理，無以整外。

translations.literal: if the inner gate is not put in order, there is no means to regulate the outer.
translations.idiomatic: When the inner offices fall into chaos, no amount of zeal on the frontier can restore order.

sentences.zh: 府丞悉署吏行能，分別白之。

translations.literal: Let the assistant governor fully record clerks' conduct and ability and report them separately.
translations.idiomatic: The deputy shall rank every clerk by talent.

sentences.zh: 賢為上，毋以富。

translations.literal: Put worth first, not wealth.
translations.idiomatic: Merit before money.

sentences.zh: 賈人百萬，不足與計事。

translations.literal: A merchant with a million cash is still not worth consulting on affairs.
translations.idiomatic: A millionaire merchant is no partner in policy.

sentences.zh: 昔孔子治魯，七日誅少正卯，今太守視事已一月矣，五官掾張輔懷虎狼之心，貪汙不軌，一郡之錢盡入輔家，然適足以葬矣。

translations.literal: In antiquity Confucius governed Lu and in seven days executed Shao Zhengmao; now the governor has been in office a month, yet the chief clerk Zhang Fu harbors a tiger-wolf heart, is corrupt and lawless, and the commandery's cash has all entered Fu's house—yet that is just enough to bury him.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius killed Shao Zhengmao in seven days; Wang Zun had been governor a month while Zhang Fu embezzled the treasury dry—enough loot to fill his grave.

sentences.zh: 今將輔送獄，直符史詣閤下，從太守受其事。

translations.literal: Now I will send Fu to prison; let the tally clerk go to the gate and receive orders from the governor.
translations.idiomatic: Fu goes to jail now; the tally clerk will take him from my hand.

sentences.zh: 丞戒之戒之！

translations.literal: Assistant, take warning, take warning!
translations.idiomatic: "Assistant governor—mark this warning!"

sentences.zh: 相隨入獄矣！」

translations.literal: You will follow him into prison!"
translations.idiomatic: "Or you will follow Zhang Fu into the same cell!"

sentences.zh: 輔繫獄數日死，盡得其狡猾不道，百萬姦臧。

translations.literal: Fu was bound in prison several days and died; the full extent of his craftiness and lack of the Way, a million in wicked spoils, was obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fu died in custody; the audit found a million in dirty coin.

sentences.zh: 威震郡中，盜賊分散，入傍郡界。

translations.literal: Awe shook the commandery; bandits scattered into neighboring commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery froze; bandits fled across the line.

sentences.zh: 豪彊多誅傷伏辜者。

translations.literal: Many of the mighty were executed or wounded and submitted to punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Magnates fell in waves to the headsman's bill.

sentences.zh: 坐殘賊免。

translations.literal: He was dismissed for cruelty and brigandage.
translations.idiomatic: He lost the post for excessive harshness.

sentences.zh: 起家，復為護羌將軍轉校尉，護送軍糧委輸。

translations.literal: He rose from commoner status again to serve as acting colonel under the General Who Protects the Qiang, escorting army grain for transport.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled from private life, he escorted grain as acting colonel under the Qiang campaign.

sentences.zh: 而羌人反，絕轉道，兵數萬圍尊。

translations.literal: The Qiang rebelled, cut the transport routes, and several ten thousand troops surrounded Zun.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang cut his road and surrounded him with tens of thousands.

sentences.zh: 尊以千餘騎奔突羌賊。

translations.literal: Zun with over a thousand horse charged and broke through the Qiang bandits.
translations.idiomatic: He punched through with a thousand riders.

sentences.zh: 功未列上，坐擅離部署，會赦，免歸家。

translations.literal: His merit was not yet reported upward; he was convicted for leaving his deployment without authority; there was an amnesty; he was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: Before his dispatch could be filed he was cashiered for abandoning his column—then amnestied home.

sentences.zh: 涿郡太守徐明薦尊不宜久在閭巷，上以尊為郿令，遷益州刺史。

translations.literal: Governor of Zhuo Xu Ming recommended that Zun should not long remain in the lanes; the emperor appointed Zun magistrate of Mei and promoted him to Inspector of Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Ming of Zhuo said waste of such talent was a crime; the court made him Mei county magistrate, then inspector of Yi.

sentences.zh: 先是，琅邪王陽為益州刺史，行部至邛郲九折阪，歎曰：「奉先人遺體，柰何數乘此險！」

translations.literal: Earlier, Wang Yang of Langye had been Inspector of Yi; on his circuit he reached the nine bends of Qionglai pass and sighed: "I carry my ancestors' leftover body—how can I repeatedly mount this danger!"
translations.idiomatic: At the nine bends of Qionglai, Wang Yang had sighed, "I carry my parents' leftover body—how can I risk this road again and again!"

sentences.zh: 後以病去。

translations.literal: Later he left because of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He quit on health grounds.

sentences.zh: 及尊為刺史，至其阪，問吏曰：「此非王陽所畏道邪？」

translations.literal: When Zun became inspector and reached that pass, he asked the clerks: "Is this not the road Wang Yang feared?"
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun asked his escort: "Is this Wang Yang's coward's hill?"

sentences.zh: 吏對曰：「是。」

translations.literal: The clerks answered: "Yes."
translations.idiomatic: They said yes.

sentences.zh: 尊叱其馭曰：「驅之！

translations.literal: Zun shouted at his charioteer: "Drive on!"
translations.idiomatic: Zun shouted at his driver, "Drive on!"

sentences.zh: 王陽為孝子，王尊為忠臣。」

translations.literal: "Wang Yang was a filial son; Wang Zun is a loyal minister."
translations.idiomatic: He shouted to his driver: "Wang Yang turned back like a filial son; Wang Zun rides through like a loyal minister."

sentences.zh: 尊居部二歲，懷來徼外，蠻夷歸附其威信。

translations.literal: Zun stayed in his inspectorate two years; he won over those beyond the frontier and the Man and Yi submitted to his awe-inspiring trust.
translations.idiomatic: Two years in Yi won back the tribes beyond the passes.

sentences.zh: 博士鄭寬中使行風俗，舉奏尊治狀，遷為東平相。

translations.literal: Erudite Zheng Kuanzhong was sent to inspect customs; he recommended Zun's administration; Zun was promoted to chancellor of Dongping.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Kuanzhong's inspection tour praised him up to chancellor of Dongping.

sentences.zh: 是時，東平王以至親驕奢不奉法度，傅相連坐。

translations.literal: At that time the King of Dongping, as close kin, was arrogant and extravagant and did not observe the law; tutors and chancellors suffered joint punishment in succession.
translations.idiomatic: Dongping's king was another pampered kinsman who broke the law and burned through chancellors.

sentences.zh: 及尊視事，奉璽書至庭中，王未及出受詔，尊持璽書歸舍，食已乃還。

translations.literal: When Zun took office he brought the imperial rescript to the courtyard; before the king had come out to receive the edict, Zun carried the rescript back to his quarters, finished eating, and then returned.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun stalked into the courtyard with the sealed edict, walked away before the king appeared, ate lunch, then came back.

sentences.zh: 致詔後，謁見王，太傅在前說相鼠之詩。

translations.literal: After delivering the edict he had audience with the king; the grand tutor stepped forward and recited the ode "The Rat."
translations.idiomatic: At the levee the grand tutor lectured him with the "Big Rat" poem from the Odes.

sentences.zh: 尊曰：「毋持布鼓過雷門！」

translations.literal: Zun said: "Do not take a cloth drum past Thunder Gate!"
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun cut him short: "Do not bang a rag drum at Thunder Gate!"

sentences.zh: 王怒，起入後宮。

translations.literal: The king, angry, rose and entered the inner palace.
translations.idiomatic: The king stormed off to the harem.

sentences.zh: 尊亦直趨出就舍。

translations.literal: Zun also went straight out to his quarters.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun marched straight out to his own lodging.

sentences.zh: 先是王數私出入，驅馳國中，與后姬家交通。

translations.literal: Earlier the king had repeatedly slipped out in private, galloped through the kingdom, and kept company with consorts' families.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Dongping had been sneaking out to carouse with his in-laws' households.

sentences.zh: 尊到官，召敕廄長：「大王當從官屬，鳴和鸞乃出，自今有令駕小車，叩頭爭之，言相教不得。」

translations.literal: When Zun reached his post he summoned and charged the stable chief: "When the great king goes out he must follow his officials and only depart when carriage bells harmonize; from now on, if anyone orders a small carriage out, kowtow and oppose it, saying that the chancellor has forbidden it."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun ordered the stable master to block any unofficial joyride—the king rode only with full escort and harmonized bells.

sentences.zh: 後尊朝王，王復延請登堂。

translations.literal: Later, when Zun had audience with the king, the king again invited him to ascend the hall.
translations.idiomatic: On a later levee the king invited him upstairs again.

sentences.zh: 尊謂王曰：「尊來為相，人皆弔尊也，以尊不容朝廷，故見使相王耳。

translations.literal: Zun said to the king: "Zun has come as chancellor; everyone offers condolences to Zun, because Zun was not tolerated at court and therefore was sent to serve as your chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun told the king, "Zun has come as chancellor; everyone offers me condolences, for I was not tolerated at court and was therefore sent to serve as your chancellor."

sentences.zh: 天下皆言王勇，顧但負貴，安能勇？

translations.literal: All under heaven say the king is brave, yet you merely rely on noble rank—how can that be courage?
translations.idiomatic: "All under heaven call your lordship brave—yet you merely rely on noble rank; how can that be courage?"

sentences.zh: 如尊乃勇耳。」

translations.literal: It is someone like Zun who is brave."
translations.idiomatic: "Men like Zun alone are brave."

sentences.zh: 王變色視尊，意欲格殺之，即好謂尊曰：「願觀相君佩刀。」

translations.literal: The king changed color and looked at Zun, intending to strike him dead, then said pleasantly: "I should like to see the chancellor's belt knife."
translations.idiomatic: The king changed color and stared as if he would strike, then said pleasantly, "I should like to see the chancellor's belt knife."

sentences.zh: 尊舉掖，顧謂傍侍郎：「前引佩刀視王，王欲誣相拔刀向王邪？」

translations.literal: Zun raised his sleeve, looked at the attendant gentleman beside him, and said: "Come forward and show the king your belt knife—does the king mean to accuse the chancellor of drawing a blade on him?"
translations.idiomatic: Zun raised his sleeve and told the attendant gentleman at his side, "Step forward and show the king your belt knife—is the king accusing the chancellor of drawing steel on him?"

sentences.zh: 王情得，又雅聞尊高名，大為尊屈，酌酒具食，相對極驩。

translations.literal: The king's intent was exposed; he had long heard of Zun's high reputation and greatly yielded to Zun; they poured wine, set out food, and drank together in the utmost joy.
translations.idiomatic: Caught out, the king remembered Wang Zun's reputation, poured wine, and made peace.

sentences.zh: 太后徵史奏尊「為相倨慢不臣，王血氣未定，不能忍。

translations.literal: The queen dowager summoned the chronicler and memorialized that Zun, as chancellor, was arrogant and not a minister; the king's blood and qi were not yet settled and could not endure it.
translations.idiomatic: The queen mother had the chronicler memorialize that Zun, as chancellor, was arrogant and not a proper minister, and that "the king's blood and qi are not yet settled and cannot bear such insult."

sentences.zh: 愚誠恐母子俱死。

translations.literal: In sincerity I fear mother and son may both die.
translations.idiomatic: "In sincerity I fear mother and son may both die."

sentences.zh: 今妾不得使王復見尊。

translations.literal: Now your handmaid cannot allow the king to see Zun again.
translations.idiomatic: "Now your handmaid cannot allow the king to see Zun again."

sentences.zh: 陛下不留意，妾願先自殺，不忍見王之失義也。」

translations.literal: If Your Majesty does not take note, your handmaid begs first to kill herself, unable to bear seeing the king lose righteousness."
translations.idiomatic: "If Your Majesty pays no heed, your handmaid begs to kill herself first—I cannot bear to watch the king abandon righteousness."

sentences.zh: 尊竟坐免為庶人。

translations.literal: Zun was in the end convicted and dismissed as a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun lost his post and rank.

sentences.zh: 大將軍王鳳奏請尊補軍中司馬，擢為司隸校尉。

translations.literal: Grand General Wang Feng memorialized requesting that Zun be appointed acting army major in headquarters and promoted to metropolitan governor.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng pulled him back as colonel on staff, then as metropolitan governor.

sentences.zh: 會南山群盜傰宗等數百人為吏民害，拜故弘農太守傅剛為校尉，將跡射士千人逐捕，歲餘不能禽。

translations.literal: It happened that several hundred bandits of the Zhongzong gang in the southern mountains harmed clerks and people; the former governor of Hongnong Fu Gang was appointed colonel and led a thousand mounted archers in pursuit; after more than a year they could not capture them.
translations.idiomatic: South Mountain outlaws defied a year-long dragnet led by Fu Gang's thousand archers.

sentences.zh: 或說大將軍鳳：「賊數百人在轂下，發軍擊之不能得，難以視四夷。

translations.literal: Someone advised Grand General Feng: "Several hundred bandits are beneath the hub of the chariot; raising troops yet failing to take them is hard to show the four barbarians.
translations.idiomatic: Someone urged Grand General Wang Feng, "Several hundred bandits sit beneath the chariot hubs; raising troops yet failing to take them shames the Han before the four barbarians."

sentences.zh: 獨選賢京兆尹乃可。」

translations.literal: You must choose a worthy metropolitan Governor."
translations.idiomatic: "You must choose a worthy metropolitan Governor."

sentences.zh: 於是鳳薦尊，徵為諫大夫，守京輔都尉，行京兆尹事。

translations.literal: Thereupon Feng recommended Zun; he was summoned as grandee remonstrant, metropolitan commandant for the capital region, and acting metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng nominated Wang Zun: remonstrant, capital commandant, acting governor.

sentences.zh: 旬月間盜賊清。

translations.literal: Within a month or so the bandits were cleared.
translations.idiomatic: The hills were quiet within a month.

sentences.zh: 遷光祿大夫，守京兆尹，後為真，凡三歲。

translations.literal: He was promoted to chamberlain for the palace attendants, acting metropolitan Governor, later confirmed—for three years in all.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to chamberlain, then full metropolitan Governor for three years.

sentences.zh: 坐遇使者無禮。

translations.literal: He was convicted for treating an agent without courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: He fell for disrespecting an imperial messenger.

sentences.zh: 司隸遣假佐放奉詔書白尊發吏捕人，放謂尊：「詔書所捕宜密。」

translations.literal: The metropolitan governor sent acting assistant Fang with an edict instructing Zun to dispatch officers to make arrests; Fang said to Zun: "Those named in the edict should be seized in secret."
translations.idiomatic: A clerk named Fang said the roundup must be secret.

sentences.zh: 尊曰：「治所公正，京兆善漏泄人事。」

translations.literal: Zun said: "In administering what is just and fair, the metropolitan Governor is good at leaking human affairs."
translations.idiomatic: Zun answered, "In administering what is just, the metropolitan yamen is expert at leaking human affairs."

sentences.zh: 放曰：「所捕宜今發吏。」

translations.literal: Fang said: "Those to be arrested should be taken now with dispatched officers."
translations.idiomatic: Fang said, "Those to be arrested should be taken now with officers you dispatch."

sentences.zh: 尊又曰：「詔書無京兆文，不當發吏。」

translations.literal: Zun again said: "The edict bears no metropolitan Governor's wording; officers should not be dispatched."
translations.idiomatic: Zun replied again, "The edict bears no metropolitan wording; officers should not be dispatched."

sentences.zh: 及長安繫者三月間千人以上。

translations.literal: Meanwhile more than a thousand persons were bound in Chang'an jails within three months.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Chang'an's jails swelled past a thousand inmates in three months.

sentences.zh: 尊出行縣，男子郭賜自言尊：「

translations.literal: When Zun went out to tour the counties, a man named Guo Ci addressed Zun, saying: "
translations.idiomatic: While touring the counties he met a man named Guo Ci, who addressed him: "

sentences.zh: 許仲家十餘人共殺賜兄賞，公歸舍。」

translations.literal: More than ten members of Xu Zhong's household jointly killed my elder brother Shang; they have returned to their house in the open."
translations.idiomatic: More than ten members of Xu Zhong's household jointly killed my elder brother Shang; they have gone home in the open."

sentences.zh: 吏不敢捕。

translations.literal: The clerks dared not arrest them.
translations.idiomatic: No constable would touch them.

sentences.zh: 尊行縣還，上奏曰：「彊不陵弱，各得其所，寬大之政行，和平之氣通。」

translations.literal: When Zun returned from touring the counties he submitted a memorial saying: "The strong do not oppress the weak; each obtains his proper place; the policy of magnanimity is practiced; the air of peace prevails."
translations.idiomatic: On his return he memorialized, "The strong do not oppress the weak; each has his proper place; the policy of magnanimity prevails and the air of peace runs free."—pure sarcasm.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫中奏尊暴虐不改，外為大言，倨嫚姍嫌，威信日廢，不宜備位九卿。

translations.literal: The imperial counsellor submitted that Zun was violent and unreformed, outwardly grandiloquent, arrogant, mocking, and suspicious; his awe-inspiring trust daily wasted away and he was unfit to stand among the Nine Ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The censor-in-chief impeached him for brutality, bluster, and loss of moral authority.

sentences.zh: 尊坐先，吏民多稱惜之。

translations.literal: Zun was convicted first; many clerks and people voiced pity for him.
translations.idiomatic: The verdict came down first; the capital mourned.

sentences.zh: 湖三老公乘興等上書訟尊治京兆功效日著。

translations.literal: The three village elders of Hu, Gongcheng Xing and others, submitted a memorial praising Zun's achievements in governing the capital as daily more evident.
translations.idiomatic: Three Hu district elders petitioned in Wang Zun's defense.

sentences.zh: 「往者南山盜賊阻山橫行，剽劫良民，殺奉法吏，道路不通，城門至以警戒。

translations.literal: "Formerly the southern-mountain bandits blocked the mountains and ran wild, plundered good people, killed law-abiding officers, cut the roads so that gates had to be guarded in alarm.
translations.idiomatic: The elders wrote that southern-mountain bandits had blocked the roads, plundered the people, and slain law-abiding officers.

sentences.zh: 步兵校尉使逐捕，暴師露眾，曠日煩費，不能禽制。

translations.literal: The colonel of foot sent to pursue exposed the army in the open, wasted days and expense, and could not seize or control them.
translations.idiomatic: The infantry colonel's campaign had exposed the army to weather and emptied the treasury without catching a single chief.

sentences.zh: 二卿坐黜，群盜寖強，吏氣傷沮，流聞四方，為國家憂。

translations.literal: Two ministers sat dismissed; the bandit gangs grew stronger; officer morale was wounded and sank; rumor spread in the four directions and became the state's worry.
translations.idiomatic: Two ministers fell in disgrace, the gangs grew bolder, and officer morale collapsed while rumor of Han weakness spread in every direction.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，有能捕斬，不愛金爵重賞。

translations.literal: At that time whoever could capture and behead them would not stint at noble rank or heavy reward.
translations.idiomatic: The court would have paid any price in gold or noble rank for a man who could break the deadlock.

sentences.zh: 關內侯寬中使問所徵故司隸校尉王尊捕群盜方略，拜為諫大夫，守京輔都尉，行京兆尹事。

translations.literal: Marquis within the passes Kuanzhong was sent to ask the summoned former metropolitan governor Wang Zun for his plan to catch the bandit hosts; he was appointed grandee remonstrant, metropolitan commandant for the capital region, and acting metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: At last the emperor recalled Wang Zun from private life, gave him the old brief as grandee and acting metropolitan governor, and turned him loose on the hills.

sentences.zh: 尊盡節勞心，夙夜思職，卑體下士，厲奔北之吏，起沮傷之氣，二旬之間，大黨震壞，渠率效首。

translations.literal: Zun exhausted his integrity and labored his heart, thought of duty morning and night, humbled his body before subordinates, spurred clerks who had fled defeat, and raised morale from its wound; within twenty days the great clique was shattered and the chiefs offered their heads.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun broke the great clique in twenty days and sent in the chieftains' heads.

sentences.zh: 賊亂蠲除，民反農業，拊循貧弱，鉏耘豪彊。

translations.literal: Rebellion and disorder were removed; the people returned to farming; he comforted the poor and weak and hoed down the mighty.
translations.idiomatic: Farmers went back to the plough; he coddled the poor and cut the magnates down.

sentences.zh: 長安宿豪大猾東市賈萬、城西萬章、翦張禁、酒趙放、杜陵楊章等皆通邪結黨，挾養姦軌，上干王法，下亂吏治，并兼役使，侵漁小民，為百姓豺狼。

translations.literal: The old bravos and great scoundrels of Chang'an—Jia Wan of the east market, Wan Zhang of the west market, Jian Zhang Jin, Jiu Zhao Fang, Yang Zhang of Duling—all formed wicked cliques, harbored criminals, flouted the king's law above and threw clerkly government into chaos below, merged and conscripted labor, preyed on the little people, and were wolves and tigers to the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: He listed the capital's fence bosses—Jia Wan, Wan Zhang, and the rest—who had terrorized the markets for twenty years—

sentences.zh: 更數二千石，二十年莫能禽討，尊以正法案誅，皆伏其辜。

translations.literal: Several chiefs at two thousand piculs had passed in twenty years without being able to capture or punish them; Zun prosecuted them according to the just law and all submitted to their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: For twenty years successive governors had failed; Wang Zun prosecuted those bravos to the full letter of the law and made the verdicts stick.

sentences.zh: 姦邪銷釋，吏民說服。

translations.literal: Wickedness melted away; clerks and people were pleased and submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Evil withered; the people cheered.

sentences.zh: 尊撥劇整亂，誅暴禁邪，皆前所稀有，名將所不及。

translations.literal: Zun unraveled the severe and set right disorder, executed the violent and forbade wickedness—things rarely seen before, beyond what famed generals achieved.
translations.idiomatic: They said his feat outdid any general's campaign.

sentences.zh: 雖拜為真，未有殊絕褒賞加於尊身。

translations.literal: Though he was appointed in full, no extraordinary praise or reward was added to Zun's person.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the court never gave him a special bonus.

sentences.zh: 今御史大夫奏尊『傷害陰陽，為國家憂，無承用詔書之意，靖言庸違，象龔滔天。』

translations.literal: Now the imperial counsellor memorializes that Zun 'injures yin and yang, brings worry on the state, shows no mind to receive and use edicts, speaks peace while acting perversity, and conspires with Gong to flood heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: Now the imperial counsellor writes that Zun "injures yin and yang, brings worry on the state, shows no mind to receive and use edicts, speaks peace while acting perversity, and conspires with Gong to flood heaven."

sentences.zh: 原其所以，出御史丞楊輔，故為尊書佐，素行陰賊，惡口不信，好以刀筆陷人於法。

translations.literal: Trace the reason: it comes out from assistant imperial counsellor Yang Fu, who was formerly Zun's clerk-scribe, by nature secretively vicious, foul-mouthed and untrustworthy, delighting in using brush and knife to trap people in the law.
translations.idiomatic: The true author of the impeachment, they said, was the assistant imperial counsellor Yang Fu, once Wang Zun's clerk, a man who nursed a petty grudge.

sentences.zh: 輔常醉過尊大奴利家，利家捽搏其頰，兄子閎拔刀欲剄之。

translations.literal: Fu once came drunk past Zun's chief slave Lijia; Lijia seized and boxed his cheeks, and Fu's nephew Hong drew a knife intending to cut Fu's throat.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Fu had once staggered past Wang Zun's head slave and been beaten; a nephew had nearly knifed him—

sentences.zh: 輔以故深怨疾毒，欲傷害尊。

translations.literal: For this reason Fu harbored deep resentment and poisonous hatred and wished to harm Zun.
translations.idiomatic: From that day Yang Fu nursed a poison grudge and framed the memorial that struck at Wang Zun.

sentences.zh: 疑輔內懷怨恨，外依公事，建畫為此議，傅致奏文，浸潤加誣，以復私怨。

translations.literal: We suspect Fu harbors resentment within and relies on public business without, drew up this proposal, pieced together the memorial text, seeped slander and added false charges to repay a private grudge.
translations.idiomatic: The elders called it a smear job built from office paper.

sentences.zh: 昔白起為秦將，東破韓、魏，南拔郢都，應侯譖之，賜死杜郵；

translations.literal: In antiquity Bai Qi as Qin general destroyed Han and Wei in the east and took Ying in the south; Marquis Ying slandered him and he was ordered to die at Du Post.
translations.idiomatic: They compared him to Bai Qi, Qin's greatest general, destroyed at Du Post by Lord Ying's whisper campaign.

sentences.zh: 吳起為魏守西河，而秦、韓不敢犯，讒人間焉，斥逐奔楚。

translations.literal: Wu Qi guarded western He for Wei so Qin and Han dared not invade; slanderers set him at odds and he was expelled to flee to Chu.
translations.idiomatic: They compared him to Wu Qi, who guarded western He until slander drove him from Wei into Chu.

sentences.zh: 秦聽浸潤以誅良將，魏信讒言以逐賢守，此皆偏聽不聰，失人之患也。

translations.literal: Qin listened to seeping slander and executed a good general; Wei believed slanderous words and drove away a worthy guardian—these are the misfortunes of partial hearing and failure to discern, the calamity of losing men.
translations.idiomatic: Qin had listened to dripping slander and killed a loyal general; Wei had believed lies and expelled a faithful guardian—both realms lost their best men.

sentences.zh: 臣等竊痛傷尊修身絜己，砥節首公，刺譏不憚將相，誅惡不避豪彊，誅不制之賊，解國家之憂，功岩職修，威信不廢，誠國家爪牙之吏，折衝之臣，今一旦無辜制於仇人之手，傷於詆欺之文，上不得以功除罪，下不得蒙棘木之聽，獨掩怨讎之偏奏，被共工之大惡，無所陳怨愬罪。

translations.literal: We your subjects privately grieve that Zun cultivated himself and kept pure, sharpened his integrity and put the duke first, lashed faults without fearing ministers and generals, punished evil without avoiding the mighty, executed uncontrollable bandits and lifted the state's worry; his achievement was steep and his duty was fulfilled, his awe-inspiring trust was not cast away—truly a claw-and-fang clerk of the state, a minister who breaks enemy assaults—yet now in one morning, guiltless, he falls into a foe's hands, wounded by slanderous writing; above he cannot use merit to cancel guilt, below he cannot receive the hearing of the thornwood court; alone he is smothered by a partial memorial of hatred and bears the great evil of Gong, with nowhere to voice grievance or declare innocence.
translations.idiomatic: The petition insisted that Wang Zun had crushed uncontrollable bandits and lifted a national crisis while slanderers quoted scripture to ruin him.

sentences.zh: 尊以京師廢亂，群盜並興，選賢徵用，起家為卿，賊亂既除，豪猾伏辜，即以佞巧廢黜。

translations.literal: Because the capital region was ruined and chaotic and bandits rose together, a worthy was chosen and summoned from commoner life to minister; once disorder was removed and the crafty submitted to guilt, he was dismissed on grounds of fawning cleverness.
translations.idiomatic: The court had plucked him from commoner obscurity to save the capital, then cashiered him as a flatterer the moment quiet returned.

sentences.zh: 一尊之身，三期之間，乍賢乍佞，豈不甚哉！

translations.literal: For one man's body, within three periods, now worthy, now fawning—is it not extreme!
translations.idiomatic: For one man's career to swing from paragon to parasite within three seasons was intolerable caprice.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『愛之欲其生，惡之欲其死，是惑也。』

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'To love them and wish them to live, to hate them and wish them to die—this is confusion.'
translations.idiomatic: They quoted Confucius: to love a man and wish him life, then hate him and wish him death, is the very definition of confusion.

sentences.zh: 『浸潤之譖不行焉，可謂明矣。』

translations.literal: 'When seeping slander does not succeed, that may be called clear-sighted.'"
translations.idiomatic: They quoted him again: a ruler who ignores seeping slander may truly be called clear-sighted."

sentences.zh: 願下公卿大夫博士議郎，定尊素行。

translations.literal: We beg that it be sent down to high ministers, grandees, erudites, and deliberation gentlemen to fix Zun's constant conduct.
translations.idiomatic: They asked a full bench to judge Wang Zun's whole career.

sentences.zh: 夫人臣而傷害陰陽，死誅之罪也；

translations.literal: For a minister to injure yin and yang is a crime deserving death by execution;
translations.idiomatic: If Wang Zun had truly unbalanced yin and yang, let him die beneath the palace watchtower.

sentences.zh: 靖言庸違，放殛之刑也。

translations.literal: 'Speaking peace while acting perversity' is a crime deserving banishment and execution.
translations.idiomatic: If he had smooth-talked the throne while acting perversely, let him suffer banishment and the axe.

sentences.zh: 審如御史章，尊乃當伏觀闕之誅，放於無人之域，不得苟免。

translations.literal: If it is truly as in the imperial counsellor's memorial, then Zun ought to suffer execution beneath the watchtower and be cast into uninhabited wastes, with no chance of leniency.
translations.idiomatic: If the charges did not hold, let the slanderers themselves face the law at the palace gate.

sentences.zh: 及任舉尊者，當獲選舉之辜，不可但已。

translations.literal: Those who nominated Zun should bear the guilt of faulty recommendation; it cannot stop with dismissal alone.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who recommended him should share the blame.

sentences.zh: 即不如章，飾文深詆以愬無罪，亦宜有誅，以懲讒賊之口，絕詐欺之俗。

translations.literal: If it does not match the memorial, yet the text was ornamented with deep slander to accuse the guiltless, there should still be punishment to check the mouths of slanderers and cut off the custom of deceit.
translations.idiomatic: If the memorial was false, punish the authors and choke off lying.

sentences.zh: 唯明主參詳，使白黑分別。」

translations.literal: We beg the enlightened ruler to deliberate in detail and make black and white distinct."
translations.idiomatic: They begged the emperor to sort truth from lies."

sentences.zh: 書奏，天子復以尊為徐州刺史，遷東郡太守。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the Son of Heaven again appointed Zun Inspector of Xuzhou and promoted him to governor of the Eastern Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor restored him to Xuzhou inspector, then governor of the Eastern Commandery.

sentences.zh: 久之，河水盛溢，泛浸瓠子金隄，老弱奔走，恐水大決為害。

translations.literal: After a long while the Yellow River swelled and overflowed, flooding and soaking the Huzi golden dike; old and weak fled in panic, fearing the water would burst in great breach and do harm.
translations.idiomatic: Years later the Yellow River threatened the Huzi levee; villagers fled the coming breach.

sentences.zh: 尊躬率吏民，投沈白馬，祀水神河伯。

translations.literal: Zun personally led clerks and people, cast in a white horse, and sacrificed to the river god He Bo.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun led the sacrifice—a white horse to the River Earl.

sentences.zh: 尊親執圭璧，使巫策祝，請以身填金隄，因止宿，廬居隄上。

translations.literal: Zun personally held jade tablets and disks, had shamans divine and pray, offered his own body to plug the golden dike, then stopped overnight and lodged in a hut on the dike.
translations.idiomatic: He swore to plug the gap with his own corpse and slept on the embankment.

sentences.zh: 吏民數千萬人爭叩頭救止尊，尊終不肯去。

translations.literal: Several tens of thousands of clerks and people vied to kowtow and beg him to stop; Zun would not leave.
translations.idiomatic: Tens of thousands begged him to come down; he refused.

sentences.zh: 及水盛隄壞，吏民皆奔走，唯一主簿泣在尊旁，立不動。

translations.literal: When the water rose high the dike broke; clerks and people all fled; only one chief clerk wept beside Zun and stood motionless.
translations.idiomatic: When the wall gave way everyone ran; one chief clerk stayed weeping at his side.

sentences.zh: 而水波稍卻迴還。

translations.literal: Yet the water waves slightly drew back and returned.
translations.idiomatic: The flood then eased and rolled back.

sentences.zh: 吏民嘉壯尊之勇節，白馬三老朱英等奏其狀。

translations.literal: Clerks and people praised the bravery of Zun's courage and integrity; the three village elders of the white horse, Zhu Ying and others, memorialized his conduct.
translations.idiomatic: The white-horse district elders reported his stand to the throne.

sentences.zh: 下有司考，皆如言。

translations.literal: It was sent down to responsible agencies for examination; all matched what was said.
translations.idiomatic: Investigators confirmed every word.

sentences.zh: 於是制詔御史：「東郡河水盛長，毀壞金隄，未決三尺，百姓惶恐奔走。

translations.literal: Thereupon an edict ordered the imperial secretary: "In the Eastern Commandery the Yellow River rose high, destroyed the golden dike, came within three feet of breaking, and the hundred surnames fled in panic.
translations.idiomatic: An edict ordered the imperial secretary: "In the Eastern Commandery the Yellow River rose high, ruined the golden dike, came within three feet of breaking, and the people fled in panic."

sentences.zh: 太守身當水衝，履咫尺之難，不避危殆，以安眾心，吏民復還就作，水不為災，朕甚嘉之。

translations.literal: The governor personally blocked the water's rush, trod the danger of inches, did not avoid peril and death, thereby settling the multitude's hearts; clerks and people returned again to their work and the water did not become disaster—We greatly approve.
translations.idiomatic: "The governor personally blocked the flood's rush, trod danger inch by inch, and did not flee death or peril, thereby steadying every heart; clerks and people returned to the levee and the water did not become disaster—We greatly approve."

sentences.zh: 秩尊中二千石，加賜黃金二十斤。」

translations.literal: Raise his rank to fully two thousand piculs and grant twenty jin of gold in addition."
translations.idiomatic: "Raise his rank to the full two thousand piculs and grant twenty jin of gold besides."

sentences.zh: 數歲，卒官，吏民紀之。

translations.literal: After several years he died in office; clerks and people recorded him.
translations.idiomatic: He died in harness; the commandery wrote his name into local lore.

sentences.zh: 尊子伯亦為京兆尹，坐耎弱不勝任免。

translations.literal: Zun's son Bo also became metropolitan Governor; he was dismissed for weakness and inability to bear the burden.
translations.idiomatic: His son Wang Bo later held the same post but was sacked for softness.

sentences.zh: 王章字仲卿，泰山鉅平人也。

translations.literal: Wang Zhang, courtesy name Zhongqing, was from Juping in Taishan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zhang, styled Zhongqing, was from Juping in Taishan.

sentences.zh: 少以文學為官，稍遷至諫大夫，在朝廷名敢直言。

translations.literal: In youth he entered office through literary study and was gradually promoted to grandee remonstrant; at court he was famed for daring to speak bluntly.
translations.idiomatic: He rose through the literary track to grandee remonstrant and a reputation for plain speech.

sentences.zh: 元帝初，擢為左曹中郎將，與御史中丞陳咸相善，共毀中書令石顯，為顯所陷，咸減死髡，章免官。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Yuan's reign he was promoted to colonel of the left guard and was friendly with imperial counsellor assistant Chen Xian; together they slandered Palace Secretary Shi Xian; Xian trapped them; Xian's sentence was commuted from death to shaved-head labor, while Zhang was dismissed from office.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yuan he joined Chen Xian against the eunuch Shi Xian; Shi Xian won—Chen nearly died, Wang was cashiered.

sentences.zh: 成帝立，徵章為諫大夫，遷司隸校尉，大臣貴戚敬憚之。

translations.literal: When Emperor Cheng ascended, Zhang was summoned as grandee remonstrant, promoted to metropolitan governor; great ministers and imperial in-laws feared and respected him.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi recalled him to metropolitan governor; the mighty walked wide of him.

sentences.zh: 王尊免後，代者不稱職，章以選為京兆尹。

translations.literal: After Wang Zun was dismissed his replacement did not fit the post; Zhang was selected metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Zun fell, Wang Zhang was picked to replace him.

sentences.zh: 時帝舅大將軍王鳳輔政，章雖為鳳所舉，非鳳專權，不親附鳳。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor's maternal uncle Grand General Wang Feng directed the government; though Zhang had been raised by Feng, he did not approve Feng's monopoly of power and did not attach himself to Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng had sponsored him, but Wang Zhang would not join the clan machine.

sentences.zh: 會日有蝕之，章奏封事，召見，言鳳不可任用，宜更選忠賢。

translations.literal: It happened that there was a solar eclipse; Zhang submitted a sealed memorial, was summoned for audience, and said Feng could not be employed and that loyal worthies should be chosen instead.
translations.idiomatic: A solar eclipse brought a sealed memorial urging Chengdi to sack Wang Feng.

sentences.zh: 上初納受章言，後不忍退鳳。

translations.literal: The emperor at first accepted Zhang's words, then could not bear to dismiss Feng.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wavered and kept his uncle.

sentences.zh: 章由是見疑，遂為鳳所陷，罪至大逆。

translations.literal: Zhang was therefore viewed with suspicion and was then trapped by Feng, his crime reaching great treason.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng struck back with a treason charge.

sentences.zh: 語在元后傳。

translations.literal: The account is in the biography of Empress Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: (His end is told in the annals of Empress Yuan.)

sentences.zh: 初，章為諸生學長安，獨與妻居。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Zhang was a student studying in Chang'an, he lived alone with his wife.
translations.idiomatic: As a student in Chang'an he lived alone with his wife.

sentences.zh: 章疾病，無被，臥牛衣中，與妻決，涕泣。

translations.literal: Zhang fell ill, had no quilt, lay in a cattleherd's straw cloak, and took leave of his wife, weeping.
translations.idiomatic: Once, feverish under a cowhide mat, he wept farewell to her.

sentences.zh: 其妻呵怒之曰：「仲卿！

translations.literal: His wife scolded him in anger, saying: "Zhongqing!
translations.idiomatic: She snapped: "Zhongqing!

sentences.zh: 京師尊貴在朝廷人誰踰仲卿者？

translations.literal: Among the honored and noble of the capital at court, who surpasses Zhongqing?
translations.idiomatic: Who in this court outranks you in promise?

sentences.zh: 今疾病困厄，不自激卬，乃反涕泣，何鄙也！」

translations.literal: Now, stricken and in straits, you do not rouse yourself but weep instead—how base!"
translations.idiomatic: A little fever and you whimper like a child!"

sentences.zh: 後章仕宦歷位，及為京兆，欲上封事，妻又止之曰：「人當知足，獨不念牛衣中涕泣時耶？」

translations.literal: Later Zhang rose through office; when he became metropolitan Governor and wished to submit a sealed memorial, his wife again stopped him, saying: "People ought to know contentment—do you not remember weeping in the cattle cloak?"
translations.idiomatic: When he prepared another sealed memorial, his wife stopped him again: "People ought to know contentment—have you forgotten weeping under the cattle mat?"

sentences.zh: 章曰：「非女子所知也。」

translations.literal: Zhang said: "This is not something a woman understands."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang answered, "That is not something a woman understands."

sentences.zh: 書遂上，果下廷尉獄，妻子皆收繫。

translations.literal: The memorial went up; indeed he was sent down to the commandant of justice's prison and his wife and children were all arrested.
translations.idiomatic: He filed anyway and landed in prison with his whole family.

sentences.zh: 章小女年可十二，夜起號哭曰：「平生獄上呼囚，素常至九，今八而止。

translations.literal: Zhang's younger daughter, about twelve years old, rose at night and wailed: "In ordinary times when prisoners are called up the count usually reaches nine; now it stops at eight.
translations.idiomatic: His younger daughter, about twelve, rose at night and wailed, "When prisoners are called up the roll always reaches nine; tonight it stopped at eight."

sentences.zh: 我君數剛，先死者必君。」

translations.literal: My lord is by nature stubborn; the one who dies first will surely be my lord."
translations.idiomatic: "My father is stubborn by nature—the ninth name will surely be his."

sentences.zh: 明日問之，章果死。

translations.literal: The next day when they inquired, Zhang had indeed died.
translations.idiomatic: By morning the guards found Wang Zhang dead in his cell.

sentences.zh: 妻子皆徙合浦。

translations.literal: His wife and children were all relocated to Hepu.
translations.idiomatic: The court banished his wife and children to Hepu on the Gulf of Tonkin.

sentences.zh: 大將軍鳳薨後，弟成都侯商復為大將軍輔政，白上還章妻子故郡。

translations.literal: After Grand General Wang Feng died, his younger brother the Marquis of Chengdu, Wang Shang, again became grand general directing the government and memorialized the emperor to return Zhang's wife and children to their native commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Shang succeeded his brother Wang Feng and won permission to bring Wang Zhang's family home.

sentences.zh: 其家屬皆完具，采珠致產數百萬，時蕭育為泰山太守，皆令贖還故田宅。

translations.literal: The family members were all safe and whole; they gathered pearls and built an estate of several million cash; at that time Xiao Yu was governor of Taishan and had them all ransomed back to their former fields and houses.
translations.idiomatic: They had grown rich diving for pearls until Xiao Yu, as governor of Taishan, helped them repurchase their old lands.

sentences.zh: 章為京兆二歲，死不以其罪，眾庶冤紀之，號為三王。

translations.literal: Zhang had been metropolitan Governor two years; he died not for his crimes—the common people thought it unjust and commemorated him; they were called the Three Wangs.
translations.idiomatic: He had ruled the capital two years and died for a crime he did not commit; the people mourned him as one of the "Three Wangs."

sentences.zh: 王駿自有傳，駿即王陽子也。

translations.literal: Wang Jun has his own biography; Jun was the son of Wang Yang.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jun—Wang Yang's son—has a separate biography in this history.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：自孝武置左馮翊、右扶風、京兆尹，而吏民為之語曰：「前有趙、張，後有三王。」

translations.literal: The appraisers say: From the time when Emperor Wu established Left Pingyi, Right Fufeng, and the metropolitan Governor, clerks and people made this saying: "Before there were Zhao and Zhang; after, the three Wangs."
translations.idiomatic: The eulogy recalls the proverb of Emperor Wu's three capital prefectures: first Zhao Guanghan and Zhang Chang, then the three governors surnamed Wang.

sentences.zh: 然劉向獨序趙廣漢、尹翁歸、韓延壽，馮商傳王尊，揚雄亦如之。

translations.literal: Yet Liu Xiang alone ordered Zhao Guanghan, Yin Wengui, and Han Yanshou; Feng Shang transmitted Wang Zun; Yang Xiong did the same.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang anthologized only Zhao, Yin, and Han; Feng Shang and Yang Xiong gave Wang Zun his own scroll.

sentences.zh: 廣漢聰明，下不能欺，延壽厲善，所居移風，然皆訐上不信，以失身墮功。

translations.literal: Guanghan was clever; those below could not deceive him; Yanshou was stern in goodness—wherever he dwelt he shifted custom—yet both impeached superiors and were untrustworthy, thereby losing themselves and destroying their merit.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Guanghan's wit left no clerk a liar; Han Yanshou's moral zeal remolded every commandery—yet each turned on his masters and threw away his fame.

sentences.zh: 翁歸抱公絜己，為近世表。

translations.literal: Wengui embraced fairness and kept himself pure—he was a model for recent generations.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Wengui wrapped the law in personal integrity and set the standard for his day.

sentences.zh: 張敞衎衎，履忠進言，緣飾儒雅，刑罰必行，縱赦有度，條教可觀，然被輕惰之名。

translations.literal: Zhang Chang was calm and ample; he trod loyalty and advanced remonstrance; he trimmed himself in Confucian polish, punishments were always enforced, pardons had proper limit, his regulations were worth studying—yet he bore the reputation of levity and laziness.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang walked the tightrope between Ru learning and relentless punishment—history still pins on him the label of a rake.

sentences.zh: 王尊文武自將，所在必發，譎詭不經，好為大言。

translations.literal: Wang Zun himself wielded civil and military arts; wherever he went he always exposed crime—deceptive and unorthodox, fond of grand pronouncements.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zun mixed bravado with battlefield nerve and never met a crisis he could not dramatize.

sentences.zh: 王章剛直守節，不量輕重，以陷刑戮，妻子流遷，哀哉！

translations.literal: Wang Zhang was rigidly upright and kept his integrity, without weighing what was light or heavy, and so fell into torture and execution; wife and children were driven far away—alas!
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zhang chose principle over survival; his kin paid the price of exile—how bitter the end.
